name: Goycoechea's Overland Expedition to Bodega
summary: 10 soldiers + sergeant march overland from SF via Bolinas and Tomales Bay to Bodega. Coast Miwok direct them to harder western route — later admitted "deceived." Territory 45 miles north of SF is "tierra incognita."
result: "Tierra incognita" — Spain's own governor admits unknown territory

name: Eliza's Activo at Bodega — Spain's Last Pacific Exploration
summary: Eliza arrives in Activo after near-starvation NW Coast voyage. Spain's last Pacific exploration. The exhausted vessel barely makes it to Bodega — the empire's reach is collapsing.
result: Last Spanish Pacific exploration — barely survived

name: Golden Gate Crossing — Borrowed Launches
summary: 32 horses ferried across the Golden Gate using launches borrowed from visiting San Blas frigates Princesa and Aranzazu. Spain cannot cross its own bay without foreign ships.
result: Spain depends on visiting vessels to cross its own frontier

name: Viceroy Orders Bodega — "Whether or Not English Are There"
summary: Revilla Gigedo orders Bodega occupied. Full fortification plan for Bodega + SF + Monterey + SD. Never executed. Supplies eventually repurposed (June 10, 1794).

name: English at Bodega "con los cañones en tierra"
summary: Arrillaga reports an English vessel anchored at Bodega with "cannons on land." Identity uncertain — Colnett traces? New vessel? Rumor? Same year as Spanish Bodega failure.
result: Foreign military presence in territory Spain cannot reach

name: Zúñiga Shows Bodega Plans to British — No Concealment
summary: San Diego commandant Zúñiga freely shows Bodega harbor plans to British officers. No concealment at all — contradicts the tight security at SF. The policy was inconsistent.
result: Concealment policy fails at San Diego

name: RAC Fur-Collecting Begins at Bodega
summary: 130 Aleut hunters aboard Kodiak. First Russian representative: Tarakanov. 40+ Russians + 150 Indians by 1809 (Argüello report). Moraga protests — ignored.
result: Permanent Russian presence established — Spain protests but cannot act

name: 130 Bidarkas in San Francisco Bay
summary: 130 Aleut bidarkas (kayaks) hunting sea otter inside San Francisco Bay. The Russians are inside Spain's premier port and Spain cannot stop them.
result: Russian hunting inside the Bay — sovereignty violated

name: Farallon Islands — Russian Sealing Station
summary: Russian sealing station on the Farallones — 18 miles off the coast of San Francisco. Aleut hunters camped seasonally. Spain/Mexico never contested it.
result: Russian territory visible from SF — unchallenged

name: Hagemeister Treaty with Kashaya Pomo
summary: Russia negotiates land cession with Kashaya Pomo chiefs — not Spanish authorities. "Allies of Russia" medals distributed 1819. Russians treat indigenous peoples as sovereign landowners because, north of the Bay, they are.
result: Russia treats Pomo as sovereign — Spain does not exist here

name: Kuskov's RAC Letters to the SF Commandant
summary: Ivan Kuskov, RAC commercial councillor at Ross, writes directly to the SF commandant "in the name of the Honorable Russian-American Company under special protection of His Majesty the Emperor of all the Russias," sending agent Slobodchikov to settle accounts and pressing for the return of captured Kodiak Aleuts.
result: Ross corresponds with SF as one sovereign to another

name: Spanish Missions Supply Kuskov at Fort Ross
summary: The Spanish missions supplying Kuskov's Russian colony — Spain's own establishments provisioning the foreign outpost on the ground it claimed. The open coast as routine economic fact, running in Spain's disfavor.
result: Spain provisions the colony it was ordered to uproot

name: Fort Ross Abandonment Resolved
summary: The RAC Main Board resolves to abandon Ross and "sell or barter for wheat to the inhabitants of San Francisco." The otter gone, Ross is liquidated — the economic close of the arc that opened with the resource.
result: Russia leaves — Ross sold to Sutter, a promoter of the US invasion
sources: Fort Ross AVPRI, 1841 abandonment resolution (via Gibson & Istomin).

name: RAC Parlamentario — Podushkin on the Chirikoff at Monterey
summary: Baranov, from Novo-Arkhangelsk, protests the "insults to the Russian Flag and the capture and keeping in cruel bondage of Russians… in the waters and the coasts of California," invokes the 1812 treaty, and sends Lt. Podushkin as a parlamentario aboard the Chirikoff to Monterey. Russia negotiating as a commercial sovereign on Spain's claimed coast.
result: Russia treats with Spain as an equal power on the California coast

name: Yanovsky's RAC Trade Overture
summary: RAC chief manager Semyon Yanovsky proposes a joint, equal-shares otter-hunt and open commerce (via Khlebnikov and the Madrid embassy; the Ilmena to supply Ross). Solá replies he cannot grant otter-hunting — only the Viceroy can. The third volume of one continuous Russo-Spanish negotiation.
result: Russia again negotiates as a commercial sovereign — Spain defers to Mexico City

name: The Suvorov at SF + the RAC Proclamation to California
summary: The RAC ship Suvorov (Lazarev) calls at San Francisco carrying a formal RAC directors' overture "to the noble and honoured Spanish inhabitants of California," invoking Russo-Spanish friendship, the 1812 treaty, and Rezanov's 1806 visit — the institutional Russian demand for the California provision trade, arriving by ship.
result: The RAC's formal trade-opening request, delivered to SF
sources: C-A 12 C478 + C479 (Suvorov 1815; passport-proclamation St. Petersburg, 12 Jul 1811).

name: Rezanov Arrives on Juno
summary: Nikolai Rezanov — Imperial Chamberlain, RAC shareholder, diplomat. Arrives at SF. Concepción Argüello courtship. Intelligence gathering on Spanish defenses. First Russian official at SF.

name: Coast Miwok Intelligence to Sal
summary: Coast Miwok informant tells Commandant Sal two ships at Bodega — soldiers "dressed like ours turned inside out." Earliest warning of foreign presence, 3 weeks before diplomatic intelligence through official channels.
result: Indigenous networks faster than imperial intelligence

name: Coast Miwok Deceive Goycoechea
summary: Coast Miwok tell Goycoechea the eastern shores are "impassable" — he takes the harder western route via Marin headlands. Later admitted he was "deceived." Information control: indigenous peoples directing Spanish military movements.

name: Coast Miwok Calibrated Diplomacy at Bodega
summary: Coast Miwok "prepared to give us battle" — choose instead to present pinole and fruit. Not submission; calibrated response. They evaluated the Spanish party and chose diplomacy.
result: Military capability held in reserve — diplomacy chosen

name: Coast Miwok Bilingual Intermediaries at Tomales
summary: Coast Miwok calling "Amico," speaking Spanish, pointing up lagoon repeating "Spaniard." Bilingual intermediaries positioning between British and Spanish empires. They know both powers and broker between them.

name: Guirmenes Intel on Bodega — "Keep This Indian Happy"
summary: Indigenous informant (Guirmenes) reports no foreign ship at Bodega for 2 years. Governor orders: "previene se tenga á ese indio contento" (keep this Indian happy). Indigenous surveillance network sustained across years — Spain depends on it.

name: Chief Vallí-éla Requests Russian Flag
summary: Chief Vallí-éla of Bodega Miwok requests Russian protection and asks Golovnin for a Russian flag. "Very glad to have Russian neighbors." Three independent accounts (Golovnin, Matiushkin, Lütke). The indigenous chief chooses his empire.
result: Indigenous imperial choice — requests Russian protection
sources: Golovnin 1979:165 via Farris 1998:6; AVPRI deep read lines 176–190.

name: Menzies Lands at Tomales Bay
summary: Menzies lands from Vancouver's ship. Finds cross made from cask stave, horse tracks from Goycoechea's expedition. Coast Miwok calling "Amico," speaking Spanish, pointing up lagoon repeating "Spaniard." British find Spanish traces in territory Spain couldn't hold.
result: British find evidence of failed Spanish expedition

name: HMS Raccoon at San Francisco
summary: British warship from Columbia River. HMS Raccoon had seized Fort Astoria Dec 1813. Needed gunpowder. Crew deserting to Spanish protection as "Roman Catholics." Written in English in the archive.
result: British warship uses SF as supply port — War of 1812 reaches Pacific

name: Kotzebue Visits on Rurik
summary: Otto von Kotzebue arrives at SF on scientific circumnavigation. Spain demands Ross abandonment. Diplomatic failure — Russia ignores the demand.
result: Spanish diplomatic protest — Russia ignores it

name: Colnett's Argonaut at Bodega
summary: British ship takes refuge during storm — cuts spars, makes repairs. Later misidentified by Spanish as active English threat. Ghost presence that haunted Spanish intelligence for years.

name: Solá to Viceroy: San Rafael + Russian Threat
summary: Same letter contains Mission San Rafael founding AND Russian threat warning. Dual purpose: health + strategic. Proves mission expansion was explicitly about territorial defense against Russia.

name: Colony Ross (Russian benchmark)
summary: The measuring stick. A single Russian-American Company outpost, founded September 1812 with about 95 Russians and 80 Alaska Native hunters. A 1813 Spanish intelligence report credited it with about 13 mounted cannon; Moraga's 1814 eyewitness reconnaissance counted 8 cannon in two corner towers with embrasures for far more. Either figure matches or exceeds the serviceable artillery of the entire San Francisco defense in the same decade.
result: One Russian fort ≈ the principal Spanish fort, in guns and in men
notes: Both the Ross figure (a Spanish estimate) and the SF figure (a Spanish self-inventory) are Spanish-sourced, so the comparison is internally consistent. The defensible claim is order-of-magnitude parity, not a precise gun-for-gun ratio.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: 1776. Northernmost military post. $1,400 total investment by 1793. Garrison: ~35 men.

name: Fort San Joaquín (Castillo de SF)
summary: 1794. Battery guarding the Golden Gate narrows. 1 cannon (burst 1792), 8 by 1793, 6 functional by 1820. Unmanned by 1800.

name: Mission San Rafael
summary: 1817. Anti-Russian buffer. 83 days after Hagemeister treaty. Founded without a boat.

name: Bodega y Quadra discovers and charts the port
summary: The schooner Sonora under Bodega y Quadra and pilot Francisco Mourelle charts the port on the return leg of the 1775 voyage. Their manuscript plano, with keyed points and soundings, is the founding document of the Spanish claim. The chart is in this site's gallery.
sources: Plano del Puerto del Capitan Bodega (LOC, gallery item loc-bq05); resurveyed 1793 (Cartas esfericas sheet 8)

name: The 1793 occupation attempt fails
summary: The year Spain finally moved to occupy Bodega: Matute's establishment attempt, mounted amid the Nootka crisis, was abandoned. The port Spain had claimed in 1775 stayed empty, and the re-survey of the same year became its monument. The full argument is the subject of the companion article.
result: Spain never occupied the port it discovered
sources: C-A 55 (Matute dossier); the 1793 re-survey, Cartas esfericas sheet 8 (gallery)
notes: Deliberately summary-level here; the article carries the argument and the full citations.

name: The 1811 Aleut otter fleet in San Francisco Bay
summary: Russian-American Company hunting reaches inside the bay itself: an Aleut baidarka fleet works San Francisco Bay for otter in 1811, the year before Ross was founded. Spain's inner waters were already a Russian hunting ground.

name: Vallejo's reconnaissance of Ross (informe reservado)
summary: M. G. Vallejo's confidential report on Ross: about 300 people, a wooden stockade, cannon in the towers, and Russian-American Company intelligence gathered through Wrangel's circle. The fullest Mexican-era picture of the colony, twenty years in.

name: Ross sold to Sutter; the Russians withdraw
summary: The Russian-American Company sells the Ross establishment to John Sutter and withdraws from California, ending the colony the Spanish had watched for thirty years without ever dislodging.
result: The Russian era ends by sale, not by force
sources: Duflot de Mofras (1844) records the post-sale coast; cf. the borderlands map's abandonment-resolved pin

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: October 3, 1810. Governor orders Moraga to investigate Russian fur-hunting at Bodega Bay. 10-day reconnaissance.
sources: Langelier & Rosen, fn. 52: 'Gabriel Moraga's Diary of his Expedition to the Port of Bodega, Oct. 3, 1810, Prov. St. Pap., XII, 276–79.' Diary survives in the Archives of California.

name: Bay Crossing — to Marin
summary: Crossed the bay from the Presidio to the Marin shore. Method uncertain — possibly native balsas or a borrowed vessel.
sources: SF Presidio had limited boat access. See boat-problem article.

name: San Rafael Area
summary: Passed through future site of Mission San Rafael. Bancroft: 'The site was probably selected on the advice of Moraga, who had several times passed it on his way to and from Bodega.'

name: Novato / Olompali Area
summary: Route north through Marin. Approximate — following the natural corridor through the Novato gap toward Petaluma Creek and the coast.
sources: Exact route not specified in diary abstract.

name: Petaluma Creek / Estuary
summary: Likely crossed or followed Petaluma Creek toward the coast. Natural route from interior Marin to Bodega Bay.

name: Bodega Bay
summary: Arrived at Bodega Bay. Found an American ship and Alaskan Indians (Aleut hunters working for the Russian-American Company). 10-day reconnaissance of the area. Wrote a diary of the expedition.
sources: Langelier & Rosen: 'finds an American ship and Alaskan Indians.' Diary: Prov. St. Pap., XII, 276–79.

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Returns after 10 days. Report confirms Russian presence and fur-hunting operations at Bodega.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: July 1812. Argüello sends Moraga with 4 men to investigate construction of a small Russian fort on the Sonoma coast. Fort Ross had been dedicated Sept. 10, 1812 — but Kuskov had been building since spring.

name: Bay Crossing — to Marin
summary: Crossed the bay to the Marin shore.

name: San Rafael Area
summary: Through future Mission San Rafael site. Moraga's repeated passage through here informed his later recommendation of the site.

name: Petaluma Creek Area
summary: North through Marin toward the coast. Approximate route.

name: Bodega Bay
summary: Passed through or near Bodega Bay en route to Fort Ross.

name: Fort Ross
summary: Moraga identifies the Russian settlement: a brigantine-anchored base with a 150-yard-square stockade and cannon. Notes the Russians' friendly manner. Fort Ross had been dedicated by Kuskov with 95 Russians and ~80 Aleut hunters.
sources: Langelier & Rosen: '150-yard-square stockade and cannon; notes the Russians' friendly manner.'

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Returns to SF. Report filed with Argüello.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: January 1813. Moraga drives 20 cattle and 3 horses to Fort Ross — practical diplomacy. Returns again in 1814 with Cadet Gervasio Argüello.

name: Bay Crossing — to Marin
summary: Crossed bay to Marin shore with livestock.

name: San Rafael Area
summary: Through future mission site, driving cattle north.

name: Bodega Bay Area
summary: Through Bodega country toward Fort Ross.

name: Fort Ross
summary: Jan. 1813: Delivered 20 cattle, 3 horses as goodwill gesture. 1814 return: accompanied by Cadet Gervasio Argüello. Wrote a report describing 40 gente de razón and many Kodiak Indians, cannon, glass windows in the commandant's house. Also carried official demands for Russian withdrawal.
sources: Langelier & Rosen: '40 gente de razón and many Kodiak Indians, cannon guarding the place, and glass windows in the commandant's house.'

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Returns to SF Presidio.

name: Golden Gate Crossing — Borrowed Launches
summary: 32 horses ferried across the Golden Gate using launches borrowed from visiting San Blas frigates Princesa and Aranzazu. Spain cannot cross its own bay without foreign ships.
result: Spain depends on visiting vessels to cross its own frontier

name: Goycoechea's Overland Expedition to Bodega
summary: 10 soldiers + sergeant march overland from SF via Bolinas and Tomales Bay to Bodega. Coast Miwok direct them to harder western route — later admitted "deceived." Territory 45 miles north of SF is "tierra incognita."
result: "Tierra incognita" — Spain's own governor admits unknown territory

name: Moraga's 1st Interior Expedition
summary: First major Central Valley expedition. Named the Sacramento River. 30+ stops, 17 villages contacted. No permanent occupation.

name: Moraga's 2nd Expedition — Sacramento Valley
summary: Second interior campaign. Again, a round trip.

name: Battle of Suisun
summary: 17 men cross Carquinez Strait by boat to attack Suisun ranchería. Suisun had killed 16 Christians over prior 3 years. Hard fight — survivors in huts preferred to "perish in the flames" rather than surrender. Moraga received royal commendations.
result: Spanish tactical victory at the frontier chokepoint

name: Moraga Investigates Russian Bodega
summary: Moraga goes by boat to Bodega. Finds 40+ Aleut hunting canoes, Russian supply ships at anchor. Reconnaissance, no confrontation. Protests to commandant.

name: Moraga Discovers Fort Ross
summary: Moraga with 4 soldiers investigates grounded vessel. Discovers Fort Ross: ~80 men, Kuskov commanding, all from Unalaska/Kamchatka. Vessel 8 leagues above Bodega. No opposition.

name: Moraga's Fort Ross Defense Assessment
summary: 40 Russians, 80 Aleuts, 12 guns (4-pounder), 3 falconets. Spanish troops: "no shirts, no pants, no hats." The most damning comparison in the archive.
result: Russians armed and supplied; Spanish naked

name: Argüello's Northern Expedition
summary: 55 men — one of the largest Spanish/Mexican expeditions in CA history. Two full days spent ferrying horses across Carquinez Strait with 4 assembled boats. A power that needs a weekend to cross a channel does not control the territory beyond it.
result: Two days to cross the Strait — not an empire moving through its own territory

name: Colnett's Argonaut at Bodega
summary: British ship takes refuge during storm — cuts spars, makes repairs. Later misidentified by Spanish as active English threat. Ghost presence that haunted Spanish intelligence for years.

name: Vancouver's 1st Visit to San Francisco
summary: HMS Discovery and Chatham anchor at SF. Warm reception from Commandant Sal. Menzies walks to Fort Point — finds one cannon "lash'd to a log of Wood." British see the weakness firsthand.
result: British assess SF defenses — one burst cannon

name: English at Bodega "con los cañones en tierra"
summary: Arrillaga reports an English vessel anchored at Bodega with "cannons on land." Identity uncertain — Colnett traces? New vessel? Rumor? Same year as Spanish Bodega failure.
result: Foreign military presence in territory Spain cannot reach

summary: Menzies lands from Vancouver's ship. Finds cross made from cask stave, horse tracks from Goycoechea's expedition. Coast Miwok calling "Amico," speaking Spanish, pointing up lagoon repeating "Spaniard." British find Spanish traces in territory Spain couldn't hold.
result: British find evidence of failed Spanish expedition

name: Vancouver's 2nd Visit — Concealment Policy
summary: Dramatic change from 1st visit. Armed guards, 2-mile limit, refused access to mission. But 8 new brass 4-pounders visible on beach, workers building battery. Spain scrambles to hide weakness and show strength simultaneously.
result: Concealment policy — Spain hides its defenses from the one power that matters

name: Zúñiga Shows Bodega Plans to British — No Concealment
summary: San Diego commandant Zúñiga freely shows Bodega harbor plans to British officers. No concealment at all — contradicts the tight security at SF. The policy was inconsistent.
result: Concealment policy fails at San Diego

name: English Launches at Santa Cruz
summary: English launches spotted off Santa Cruz coast, withdrew. Sgt. Pedro Amador sent as reinforcement. Soldier Mesa punished for misreporting.

name: English Shipwreck at Point Reyes / Farallones
summary: Ship sinks near Farallones. Indigenous informants report two uniformed Englishmen at Point Reyes. English naval presence at the Bay entrance during wartime. The report comes through indigenous intelligence networks.
result: English at the Bay entrance — indigenous networks provide the warning

name: English Vessel at Santa Cruz
summary: English ship arrives at Santa Cruz port, 60 miles from SF Bay. Vallejo coordinates with alcalde — no military officer available. Thin defense exposed.

name: English Corsair Campaign — Gulf of California
summary: 4 English frigates (22–24 guns each) raided Gulf of California shipping. Ransomed ships, captured brigs. Governor Arrillaga: "sin ningunos elementos de defensa." Maritime reinforcement route to California contested.
result: English naval dominance — supply route threatened

name: HMS Raccoon at San Francisco
summary: British warship from Columbia River. HMS Raccoon had seized Fort Astoria Dec 1813. Needed gunpowder. Crew deserting to Spanish protection as "Roman Catholics." Written in English in the archive.

name: Farallon Islands — Russian Sealing Station
summary: Russian sealing station on the Farallones — 18 miles off the coast of San Francisco. Aleut hunters camped seasonally. Spain/Mexico never contested it.
result: Russian territory visible from SF — unchallenged

name: Kotzebue Visits on Rurik
summary: Otto von Kotzebue arrives at SF on scientific circumnavigation. Spain demands Ross abandonment. Diplomatic failure — Russia ignores the demand.
result: Spanish diplomatic protest — Russia ignores it

name: Golovnin on Kamchatka at SF
summary: Captain Golovnin arrives at SF on world circumnavigation aboard Kamchatka. Same season as Russian Kutuzov and American Ontario. Three-power naval convergence in one month.
result: Three-power convergence: Russia + Russia + America in SF at once

name: Russian Kutuzov at SF
summary: RAC vessel Kutuzov at SF alongside Golovnin's warship and American Ontario. Spanish garrison watches foreign warships parade through their port.

name: Vasiliev Corvettes at SF
summary: Two Russian warships anchor at SF — sailing past the fortress that had 6 functional cannons and no grapeshot. Argüello follows orders to receive them "cordially and hospitably."
result: Russian warships pass indefensible fortress — received as guests

name: Hagemeister Treaty with Kashaya Pomo
summary: Russia negotiates land cession with Kashaya Pomo chiefs — not Spanish authorities. "Allies of Russia" medals distributed 1819. Russians treat indigenous peoples as sovereign landowners because, north of the Bay, they are.
result: Russia treats Pomo as sovereign — Spain does not exist here

name: Battle of Punta de Guijarros — Lelia Byrd
summary: First naval engagement between a Spanish battery and foreign vessel. American smuggling + armed rescue + artillery duel. Shaler's crew put pistols to Spanish guards' chests. Ship escaped under grapeshot fire. Spanish soldiers were complicit in the otter skin trade.
result: First foreign attack on California — American smugglers

name: American Frigate at SF — Troop Crisis
summary: Damaged American frigate anchors in SF port. Argüello forced to pull invalidos (retired soldiers) from San José to augment escorts. Chronic troop shortage: presidio can't staff itself when a foreign ship appears.
result: One foreign ship exposes garrison collapse

name: USS Ontario at San Francisco
summary: American warship Ontario at SF — same month as Golovnin's Kamchatka and RAC Kutuzov. Three nations' vessels in the Bay simultaneously. Spain watches.

name: Mercury Trial — Capt. Eyres
summary: Boston captain, 8 years trading with Russians and NW Coast Indians. Testimony at Santa Barbara. American commercial penetration of the coast — trading network connecting Boston, Russian Alaska, and California.

name: Anglo-American Vessel Blockades Guaymas
summary: Brig "Cesak," Captain "Clarick," raised American flag, fired 3 cannon shots, blockaded Guaymas. American aggression on Pacific coast in same period as Bouchard.
result: American naval aggression on Pacific coast

name: Bouchard's Attack on Monterey
summary: Argentine privateer: Argentina (38 guns) + Santa Rosa (26 guns), ~285 crew. 400 men landed. Presidio burned. Only time a California capital fell to a foreign enemy. Solá retreated to Salinas with archives + 6,000 cartridges.
result: MONTEREY FALLS — only foreign capture of a California capital

name: Bouchard at Refugio
summary: Bouchard lands, plunders Ortega's rancho, kills cattle. 3 insurgents captured in ambush: Lt. William Taylor (Boston), Martin Romero (Paraguay), Mateo José Pascual.
result: Ranch plundered; 3 captured by Spanish ambush

name: Bouchard at Santa Barbara — Guerra's Bluff
summary: Bouchard anchors. Guerra bluffs with marching troops trick — soldiers march around a hill repeatedly to look like a large army. Prisoner exchange. No fighting.

name: Bouchard at San Juan Capistrano
summary: Bouchard lands. Minor damage — straw houses burned, wine spilled. 4 insurgent deserters surrender.

name: La Pérouse at Monterey
summary: First foreign account of Spanish military capacity. Governor Fages tells La Pérouse "North California has no northern boundary at all — it extends to Mount Saint Elias." Reality: 282 soldiers for 800+ leagues of coast, 25 missions, ~50,000 Indians, only 10,000 Christian. Continental claim vs. actual control — 7 years before Nootka.
result: France sees through the fiction — 282 soldiers for 800 leagues

name: Coast Miwok Intelligence to Sal
summary: Coast Miwok informant tells Commandant Sal two ships at Bodega — soldiers "dressed like ours turned inside out." Earliest warning of foreign presence, 3 weeks before diplomatic intelligence through official channels.
result: Indigenous networks faster than imperial intelligence
