name: Guirmenes Intel on Bodega — "Keep This Indian Happy"
summary: Indigenous informant (Guirmenes) reports no foreign ship at Bodega for 2 years. Governor orders: "previene se tenga á ese indio contento" (keep this Indian happy). Indigenous surveillance network sustained across years — Spain depends on it.

name: Suisun Resist Moraga at Carquinez
summary: Suisun warriors fight Moraga at the Strait. Had killed 16 Christians over prior 3 years. This is a military frontier — Spain fights across it, does not govern beyond it.
result: Indigenous military control of the Strait frontier

name: Suispaques Evade Cabo Ortega
summary: Cabo Ortega dispatched to capture Suispaques/Guispaques — returned without success. Indigenous captain later offered to return fugitives himself — on his own terms.
result: Indigenous autonomy — Spanish military cannot operate here

name: Chief Vallí-éla Requests Russian Flag
summary: Chief Vallí-éla of Bodega Miwok requests Russian protection and asks Golovnin for a Russian flag. "Very glad to have Russian neighbors." Three independent accounts (Golovnin, Matiushkin, Lütke). The indigenous chief chooses his empire.
result: Indigenous imperial choice — requests Russian protection
sources: Golovnin 1979:165 via Farris 1998:6; AVPRI deep read lines 176–190.

name: Gualinela's Armed Resistance — 200 Men
summary: Chief Gualinela of Tiutume ranchería (Bodega), 43 people. 200 armed men — heathen + Christian neophytes from Mission San Rafael — band together to resist Mexican troops. Cross-mission alliance.
result: Armed indigenous coalition — 40 years of continuity at Bodega

name: Sal's Boundary Letter — "English Want SF Bay for a Boundary"
summary: Sal to Arrillaga: "Segun sus opiniones, quieren los ingleses la boca de este puerto por línea divisoria." Also reports one burst cannon, two English frigates at Bodega. Intelligence from Bodega y Quadra at Nootka.
result: THESIS-CRITICAL — the boundary claim stated explicitly

name: SF Cannon Burst — Sole Weapon Destroyed
summary: Cannon exploded during saint's day firing. 10 pieces, fragments reached church 125 varas. Fired by Soldado Arriola. San Francisco's sole defensive weapon destroyed before the Nootka crisis peaked.

name: Arrillaga: "Presidios Are Indefensible — English Said So"
summary: "Los Presidios de Alta Cal. están indefensos, cuya circunstancia ha sido notada por los mismos ingleses... no pueden resistir contra una invasion." The governor's own admission to his viceroy.
result: THESIS-CRITICAL — Spain admits its own weakness

name: Arrillaga: "Tierra Incognita"
summary: "El grande rodeo y tierra incognita." Territory 45 miles north of SF is literally unknown to the Spanish governor after 24 years in California.
result: THESIS-CRITICAL — Spain does not know its own territory
sources: bodega-failure-1793; Prov. St. Pap., MS., xxi. 113.

name: Viceroy Orders Bodega — "Whether or Not English Are There"
summary: Revilla Gigedo orders Bodega occupied. Full fortification plan for Bodega + SF + Monterey + SD. Never executed. Supplies eventually repurposed (June 10, 1794).

name: Fiscal: "Fortifications Would Not Prevent a Landing"
summary: Crown fiscal officer declares defense should be naval; land fortifications useless. Devastating assessment from within the imperial bureaucracy itself.
result: Spain's own bureaucracy declares California indefensible

name: Yerba Buena Battery Abandoned
summary: Argüello reports Yerba Buena Battery unmanned — only a daily visit by the vigía. SF garrison: 1 cabo, 4 artilleros, 3 cabos, 10 soldados. Barely sufficient for Presidio + fort. The key battery controlling the narrows has no one in it.

name: Pedro de Nava: Isolation Was Deliberate Policy
summary: Comandante General of the Provincias Internas explicitly rejects overland California–New Mexico road. Cites 1786 prohibition. California's territories "useless." Indigenous nations form impassable barrier. The only route is the Sea of Cortez.
result: THESIS-CRITICAL — isolation was policy, not accident

name: Fortress San Joaquín in Collapse
summary: Only 8 cannons (2 non-functional), no grapeshot, no forge, no hot-shot furnace, wadding falling apart, cartridges irreplaceable. Two years after Bouchard proved the vulnerability — still not repaired.

name: Solá to Viceroy: San Rafael + Russian Threat
summary: Same letter contains Mission San Rafael founding AND Russian threat warning. Dual purpose: health + strategic. Proves mission expansion was explicitly about territorial defense against Russia.

name: The 1793 Bodega Occupation Attempt — Matute / Aránzazu
summary: The occupation the whole story turns on. On the Viceroy's order to "reconocer y ocupar dicho Puerto, quitando á extranjeros ese abrigo inmediato á los Presidios," an expedition under Lt. Juan Bautista Matute aboard the Aránzazu was to plant a Spanish establishment at Bodega. Matute reported he "no verificaría aquel año el establecimiento" — the attempt collapsed for want of imperial capacity, not suitable ground.
result: The establishment that did not happen — Spain's claim north of SF never occupied
sources: C-A 55 Doc 335 (Matute/Aránzazu); occupation order C-A 6 Doc 192 (Revillagigedo, 6 Jul 1793) & C-A 15 Doc 515 (Arrillaga, 16 Jul 1793).

name: Bodega Establishment Abandoned
summary: Sal reports that "no se efectuará el establecimiento del puerto de Bodega," and that the articles destined for Bodega have been reassigned to the San Francisco presidio. The closing bracket on the 1793 order-and-attempt.
result: The Bodega plan formally dead — supplies redirected to SF

name: Wilcocks Calls at Russian Bodega — and the Annexation Plot
summary: The American trader James Smith Wilcocks calls at "un establecimiento ruso llamado Bodega" — "los rusos no quisieron comprar nada" — then sells Gov. Solá $700 of blankets for the four presidios. In 1817 he is accused of plotting to "levantarme con las Californias para integrarlas a los anglo-americanos" — the earliest dated articulation of the U.S.-annexation fear the open coast invited.
result: Bodega as a (failed) commercial port in the US–Russian–Spanish triangle

name: Elliot de Castro / Ilmena Seized at Refugio
summary: The Russian brig Ilmena's shore party — John Elliot de Castro, an American, an Aleut and others — is seized at Ortega's Refugio rancho, the coast's otter-smuggling rendezvous. A $2,000 ransom is offered and refused. Spain/Mexico can catch the people but not stop the trade.
result: The Mexican-era sequel — Refugio as a second Bodega-style keyhole

name: RAC Parlamentario — Podushkin on the Chirikoff at Monterey
summary: Baranov, from Novo-Arkhangelsk, protests the "insults to the Russian Flag and the capture and keeping in cruel bondage of Russians… in the waters and the coasts of California," invokes the 1812 treaty, and sends Lt. Podushkin as a parlamentario aboard the Chirikoff to Monterey. Russia negotiating as a commercial sovereign on Spain's claimed coast.
result: Russia treats with Spain as an equal power on the California coast

name: Kuskov's RAC Letters to the SF Commandant
summary: Ivan Kuskov, RAC commercial councillor at Ross, writes directly to the SF commandant "in the name of the Honorable Russian-American Company under special protection of His Majesty the Emperor of all the Russias," sending agent Slobodchikov to settle accounts and pressing for the return of captured Kodiak Aleuts.
result: Ross corresponds with SF as one sovereign to another

name: Spanish Missions Supply Kuskov at Fort Ross
summary: The Spanish missions supplying Kuskov's Russian colony — Spain's own establishments provisioning the foreign outpost on the ground it claimed. The open coast as routine economic fact, running in Spain's disfavor.
result: Spain provisions the colony it was ordered to uproot

name: Fort Ross Abandonment Resolved
summary: The RAC Main Board resolves to abandon Ross and "sell or barter for wheat to the inhabitants of San Francisco." The otter gone, Ross is liquidated — the economic close of the arc that opened with the resource.
result: Russia leaves — Ross sold to Sutter, a promoter of the US invasion
sources: Fort Ross AVPRI, 1841 abandonment resolution (via Gibson & Istomin).

name: Yanovsky's RAC Trade Overture
summary: RAC chief manager Semyon Yanovsky proposes a joint, equal-shares otter-hunt and open commerce (via Khlebnikov and the Madrid embassy; the Ilmena to supply Ross). Solá replies he cannot grant otter-hunting — only the Viceroy can. The third volume of one continuous Russo-Spanish negotiation.
result: Russia again negotiates as a commercial sovereign — Spain defers to Mexico City

name: The Suvorov at SF + the RAC Proclamation to California
summary: The RAC ship Suvorov (Lazarev) calls at San Francisco carrying a formal RAC directors' overture "to the noble and honoured Spanish inhabitants of California," invoking Russo-Spanish friendship, the 1812 treaty, and Rezanov's 1806 visit — the institutional Russian demand for the California provision trade, arriving by ship.
result: The RAC's formal trade-opening request, delivered to SF
sources: C-A 12 C478 + C479 (Suvorov 1815; passport-proclamation St. Petersburg, 12 Jul 1811).

name: British Squadron Sounds San Diego Harbor
summary: A sworn 1799 deposition: a British naval squadron awaiting the Manila galleon, with 22 Peru prizes, states a plan to winter at San Diego — whose harbour they had already secretly sounded c.1797, "entered in their boats one moonlit night, sounded the whole harbour," "instruidos por los planos de Nutka." The open coast as an actual reconnaissance ground.
result: San Diego's harbor already mapped by the enemy — the threat the 1793 order feared

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: 1776. Northernmost military post. $1,400 total investment by 1793. Garrison: ~35 men.

name: Fort San Joaquín (Castillo de SF)
summary: 1794. Battery guarding the Golden Gate narrows. 1 cannon (burst 1792), 8 by 1793, 6 functional by 1820. Unmanned by 1800.

name: Presidio of Monterey
summary: 1770. Capital of Alta California. Burned by Bouchard 1818.

name: Presidio of San Diego
summary: 1769. First presidio. Punta de Guijarros battery.

name: Mission San José
summary: 1797. Staging base for Central Valley expeditions.

name: Mission San Rafael
summary: 1817. Anti-Russian buffer. 83 days after Hagemeister treaty. Founded without a boat.

name: Mission San Francisco Solano (Sonoma)
summary: 1823. Northernmost mission. Altimira: "you will all be Russians."

name: Pueblo de San José
summary: 1777. First civilian settlement in Alta California.

name: Villa de Branciforte (Santa Cruz)
summary: 1797. Third and last pueblo. Named for Viceroy Branciforte. Planned as strategic settlement near Santa Cruz mission.

name: Santa Eulalia (Cordelia)
summary: ~1824. Mission Solano farming outpost — house for padre, corral, neophyte in charge. Suisun Valley.

name: Port Rumiantsev (Bodega Bay)
summary: ~1807. RAC fur-collecting base. First representative: Tarakanov. Permanent.

name: Fort Ross (Metini)
summary: 1812. Ivan Kuskov. 95 Russians, ~80 Aleut. 12 guns, 3 falconets. No Spanish opposition.

name: Farallon Islands Sealing Station
summary: ~1812. 18 mi off SF coast. Seasonal Aleut camps. Unchallenged.

name: Kostromitinov Ranch
summary: 1833. Russian River near Willow Creek. ~100 acres wheat by 1841.

name: Khlebnikov Ranch
summary: 1833. Upper Salmon Creek valley, ~5 mi E of Bodega. Largest ranch — bakery, forge, tobacco shed.

name: Chernykh Ranch
summary: ~1833. Near Graton (Purrington Creek). First vineyard in Sonoma County.

name: Battle at San Diego Mission
summary: First armed clash in Alta California. Spanish exploring party attacked at Cosoy. 1 Spaniard killed (blacksmith José Manuel Robles), 3 natives killed.

name: Destruction of San Diego Mission
summary: 800–1,000 attackers from 9+ rancherías; dual-axis plan (mission + presidio). Defenders: Cpl. Rocha + 3 soldiers, 2 friars, 3 artisans = 11 men. Padre Jayme martyred (18 arrow wounds). Blacksmith Arroyo killed. Carpenter Urselino died of wounds. Cpl. Rocha fired muskets all night from adobe enclosure.
result: INDIGENOUS TACTICAL VICTORY — first successful mission destruction
sources: Fuster, Registro de Defunciones (eyewitness). Ortega, Informe Nov. 30, 1775. Bancroft I, 250-254. C-A 1 (Prov. St. Pap. i.228-32). C-A 15, n5 (ranchería list).

name: Carrillo's Punishment at Alocuachomi
summary: Punitive raid near San Juan Capistrano. 3 natives killed.

name: Ambush at San Juan (Briones killed)
summary: Midnight ambush near San Diego. Corporal Briones killed.

name: Carrillo's Raid on Pamó
summary: Dawn assault on Pamó ranchería. 4 chiefs executed — first public execution in California.

name: Tenoqui Attack on Prospecting Soldiers
summary: Santa Bárbara backcountry. 2 soldiers killed, Spanish driven off.

name: San Diego Uprising: Ranchería Participant List
summary: Full intelligence from C-A 15: 13+ rancherías identified. Gentile woman warned of second attack planned.

name: Battle at La Ciénega
summary: From hoja de servicios of Sargento Verdugo: under Capt. Rivera, "estuvieron el ataque que hicieron los gentiles en el parage de la Ciénega, en que se mataron 3."

name: Toypurina's Revolt at San Gabriel
summary: MAJOR CONSPIRACY. Toypurina — female war leader of the Tongva (Gabrieleño) — organized multi-village uprising against Mission San Gabriel with neophyte alcalde Nicolás José. Plan to kill padres and soldiers. Betrayed by informant before attack could succeed. Toypurina and co-conspirators arrested. She was exiled to Mission San Carlos Borromeo. One of the most important indigenous military actors in California history.
result: Conspiracy foiled — Toypurina arrested and exiled
sources: Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 247–249. Bancroft I. Hugo Reid letters.

name: Yuma Revolt (Colorado River Massacre)
summary: DECISIVE YUMA VICTORY. 2 mission-pueblos (La Purísima Concepción, San Pedro y San Pablo) destroyed. 46+ dead including 4 Franciscan friars. Overland route from Sonora to California closed for 40+ years.
result: DECISIVE YUMA VICTORY — overland route closed
sources: Arricivita, Crónica Seráfica (1792). Bancroft I. C-A 22.

name: Death of Rivera y Moncada
summary: Sub-engagement of Yuma Revolt. Capt. Fernando de Rivera y Moncada and all 11–12 soldiers killed at separate camp on east bank. Rivera was the military governor of the Californias.
sources: C-A 22, n300, pp. 286–88. Neve to Com. Gral.

name: Fages at Ruins of Purísima
summary: Fages arrives at "arruinado pueblo de la Purísima." Yumas have fled. Communicates with Romeu and Fueros.

name: Yuma Pacification Strategy
summary: Fages proposes withdrawing troops to let Yumas return to agriculture, then apprehending ringleaders.

name: Rivera's Final Expedition (written before massacre)
summary: Neve writes from San Gabriel: 17 families departed Loreto under Alférez Ramón Laso. Sargento Juan José Robles + 12 soldiers heading to Colorado to meet Rivera — who would be killed 2 months later.

name: Colorado River Attacks (3 incidents)
summary: Gentiles attacked 3 times, killed 5 people on the Colorado River frontier. Eastern boundary violence.

name: Colorado River: 11 of 21 Killed
summary: "De los 21 que eran se mataron allí once, y que 10 eran los fugados." Fr. Señan reports 10 killed. Solá orders vigía on Colorado River Indians.

name: Fages — El Canelo Skirmish
summary: First engagement of Fages' punitive expedition after Yuma Revolt. ~1 hour of fighting at El Canelo. 3 volunteers and 2 presidials wounded. Rescued 2 captive women and a child from Yuma captivity.
sources: Fages, Colorado River Campaign diary (Priestley, 1913). Vault: sources/fages-colorado-river-campaign-1781.md

name: Fages — Battle of October 20
summary: Major engagement of Fages' Colorado River campaign. Killed 25+ Yumas including Palma's son and brother (badly wounded). Previously ransomed 48 captives (Oct 18), 14 more (Oct 19). Officers voted unanimously to retreat Oct 21 despite Fages' intent to continue.
result: Spanish tactical victory — 25+ Yuma killed, 62 captives ransomed
sources: Fages, Colorado River Campaign diary (Priestley, 1913). Vault: sources/fages-colorado-river-campaign-1781.md

name: Sacalanes Attack on Neophyte Party
summary: East Bay hills, Sacalan territory (Alameda Co. between Oakland and Mission SJ). Dantí sent ~30 neophytes (no soldiers) across the bay to recover fugitives. Sacalanes attacked, killed 8–10. Gov. Borica refused retaliation.

name: Briones vs. Tulares Ranchería
summary: Tulare Valley edge, near SLO. Punitive raid; chief captured.

name: Amador vs. Sacalanes/Cuchillones
summary: Sacalanes ranchería, ~6 leagues from Mission SJ, in redwoods (palos colorados) and sierras — probably Sunol/Niles Canyon area. Sgt. Pedro Amador with 22 soldiers vs. ~50 Sacalanes/Cuchillones. Sacalanes dug pit traps (pozos) to neutralize cavalry — forced Spanish to dismount and fight hand-to-hand for 2+ hours. 7–8 killed.
result: Spanish victory — but innovative indigenous counter-cavalry tactics

name: Castro's Night Siege at SJB
summary: Night siege of Mission San Juan Bautista. 1 chief killed, 1 soldier wounded.

name: Amador's Raid from Santa Clara
summary: Hills east of Santa Clara. Chief killed, weapons broken, captives taken.
sources: Bancroft II. PP 0254 (Alberni orders 12 armed men for Amador).

name: Pacheco's Fight near Santa Clara
summary: 5 gentiles killed near Santa Clara.

name: Capture of Chief Guchapa
summary: Cholam village. Spanish captured chief; negotiated release.

name: Attack on Padre Cueva
summary: Ranchería 10–15 miles east of Mission San José, in Asisines/Luecheas territory (Diablo Range foothills). Spanish driven back.

name: Fight at Carquinez Strait
summary: Carquinez Strait narrows. 12–15 Mission SJ neophytes killed by gentiles controlling the strait crossing.

name: Ranchería Attack Near San José
summary: 6 leagues from San José. Multi-day pursuit. 15 volunteers + 18 soldiers. 11 killed, 31 captured.

name: San Juan Bautista Wheat-Field Arson
summary: INDIGENOUS ECONOMIC WARFARE. Wheat crop burned. Moraga captured capitanejo from Santa Cruz (pursued 3 years). Signs of sublevación.

name: Amador's Failed Attack at San José
summary: "Salió nulo el proyecto de atacar los Gentiles." Failed military operation by Sargento Pedro Amador.

name: Fugitive Neophytes: Sacalan & Cuchillones
summary: 3 Christians fled Mission SF to their lands: 1 Sacalan, 2 Cuchillones. Tribal identification of fugitives.
sources: C-A 9, n30, p. 49. Fray Martín Landaeta.

name: Amador Expedition with Captives
summary: Amador reports on expedition from SF jurisdiction. Parte y diario original. Awaits orders about "la presa que recogió" (captives).

name: Indian Killed at Monterey Presidio
summary: Indian Choychi grabbed musket at 2 AM, wounded guard Quesadas with bayonet. Soldiers entered; Marín struck him on head — died 1½ hours later "echando sangre con espuma por la boca."

name: Murder of Christian Indian Toribio
summary: Padre sent Christians to fetch gentile woman. Old gentile man + 2 sons killed Toribio — suspected him of poisoning ("enyerbado") their daughter who died at mission. Marcos Briones dispatches soldiers.

name: Expedition against Tulares Gathering
summary: Pursued fugitives, couldn't find them. Indian woman reported gentiles gathering "heading toward the Tulares." Plans to return "to pacify the gathering of Gentiles."

name: Amador Expedition to el Colorado
summary: Argüello orders Amador: "A lo gentiles no se los violente para hacerlos de sus rancherías." Gentiles fled "porque decían que querían bautizarlos" — feared forced baptism.

name: False Alarm: Attack on SLO, SM, Concepción
summary: Rumored multi-mission attack proved false. Viceroy Azanza "queda impuesto de la falsedad de la noticia."

name: Tulares: Borica Orders Avoidance
summary: "No conviene de ningún modo mandar Neofitos ni tropa á los Tulares... por ser muchísimos los Gentiles y exponerse á una pelea fuerte." Sargento Macario Castro sent with Indian guides instead.

name: Peralta's Failed Expedition from Santa Clara
summary: Cabo Luis Peralta (future grantee of Rancho San Antonio — Oakland/Berkeley/Alameda) "did not have satisfactory results for lack of good guides." Can't navigate into indigenous territory.

name: Report at Pizcache Village
summary: No combat. Oral memory of prior battle: "Other soldiers from the other side of the mountains appeared about twenty years ago." ~200 souls, chief Sujoyucomu.

name: Armed Confrontation at Río Guadalupe
summary: Tuolumne River area. 40 armed Indians with feather flag. Standoff; no casualties.

name: Encounter at Taulamne
summary: Tuolumne caves. ~200 souls on steep cliffs, inaccessible. Indians live in caves. Refused to descend. Said "soldiers killed and captured people." Armed passive resistance.

name: Skirmish at Apaglamenes/Tationes
summary: Near Grayson, Stanislaus County. 30 armed heathen on opposite bank, "painted, bedecked with feathers." 12 shots fired. One Indian hit. "Soldier Morales came into the skirmish with half his chin shaved."

name: Dawn Assault at Pescadero/Cholvones
summary: Delta — Old River area. "Before dawn we assaulted a village." 15 San José Christians recovered, 18 heathen men captured, 51 women (released — "went away very happy"). 2 prisoners escaped.

name: Battle of Suisun
summary: MAJOR ENGAGEMENT. Alf. Moraga, 17 cavalry vs. ~120 Sespesuña/Suisun warriors. Crossed Carquines Strait by boat. Hard fight — 18 captured (released, expected to die). Survivors in 3 huts: 2 stormed, all killed; 3rd set on fire — "bravely preferred to perish in the flames." 4 Spanish wounded (23.5% rate) — highest in any Spanish victory. Royal Order from Spain praised "glorious action." Moraga promoted; Herrera + Soto made sergeants.
result: Spanish tactical victory — highest casualty rate of any Spanish victory
sources: Prov. Rec. IX.122-3 (C-A 25). Prov. St. Pap. XIX.314 (C-A 12). Bancroft II, 91-92. Vallejo, Recuerdos, i.131-5.

name: Tense Encounter with Tauquimenes
summary: Sacramento River branch, delta. Standoff; no combat.

name: Soto's Battle
summary: LARGEST PITCHED BATTLE IN COLONIAL CA. Sgt. Soto + 12 soldiers from SF (by boat) + 100 Mission San José auxiliaries vs. ~1,000 warriors from 4 united rancherías (Unsumnes). Warriors pre-positioned on marshy island, women/children removed. "Fell upon the soldiers at landing." 3-hour battle. Soto "was at one time doubtful of the result, so reliant were the foes on their numbers, so careless of life." 1 neophyte killed (Alcalde Julio); many indigenous dead. Not a single fugitive captured.
result: Spanish tactical / INDIGENOUS STRATEGIC VICTORY — largest single-engagement indigenous army in CA
sources: Soto, Expedicion Militar del Sargento, 1813, MS. (in Argüello report). S. José Lib. Mision, 25. Bancroft II, 324-5. C-A 12 (Prov. St. Pap. XIX.334-348). Cook, Colonial Expeditions, 265-277.

name: Pico's Fight at San Joaquin Crossing
summary: San Joaquin River between Dos Palos and Los Banos. Day 9: armed heathen crossing river, Cpl. Juarez engaged — killed 2. Day 10: Indians came to attack camp at dawn. Line of battle formed. Interpreter asked what they wanted — "to fight." After firing, troops dismounted and pursued through brush. 3 killed, 1 captured. Dead included a Christian from Santa Cruz who was "the one most determined" to kill the soldiers.

name: Ortega's Raid on Tache
summary: Tache village, Kings River, not far west of Lemoore (Cook note [2]). Ortega attacked at dawn but village deserted — fugitive Indians had warned them "the soldiers were coming to kill them at the point of the lance." Found 3 Soledad horses. Followed into tule swamp but could not reach inhabitants.
sources: Ortega diary (Cook, pp. 267–269). Cook note [2]: Tachi territory west of Tulare Lake, villages near Lemoore.

name: Pico's Raid on Cheneche
summary: Cheneche village near mouth of Mariposa Creek, north of Los Banos, on the San Joaquin River. Pre-dawn assault at 4 AM. 66 captured (Christians and heathen). Released 4 men and 12 crippled old women. "Majority of the people escaped because this village is in a bad location." Found 7 live horses and 5 recently dead.
result: Mostly escaped — bad terrain for pursuit
sources: Pico diary (Cook, pp. 270–271). Cook note [9]: Cheneches near mouth of Mariposa Creek.

name: Tapee Horse Slaughter
summary: Village of Tapee (Tape), south of the great bend of the San Joaquin River, ~20 miles west of Fresno (Cook note [9]). Pico surrounded village on both sides of river. Found 238 RECENTLY KILLED ANIMALS, "a great deal of meat quartered and dried," and 16 live animals shot with arrows or badly treated. "From Tapee to Cheneches... more than 500 dead animals." LARGEST INDIGENOUS ECONOMIC WARFARE INCIDENT.
result: INDIGENOUS ECONOMIC WARFARE — 238+ horses killed, 500+ in region
sources: Pico diary (Cook, pp. 275–276). Cook note [9]: Tape ~20 mi. W of Fresno, south of SJ great bend.

name: Search for Malim Village
summary: Junction of Mariposa Creek and the San Joaquin River, near Los Banos. Guide (from Malim village) told them he knew the way, then when they arrived said he didn't know where it was — "but he did know that this was his country and we were hunting for his village." Guide given 10 lashes. Village found but inhabitants had escaped. 2 Christians from Santa Cruz captured.
sources: Pico diary (Cook, pp. 277–278). Cook note [9]: Malim between Cheneches and Nupchenches along SJ River.

name: Martinez Incident at Bubal
summary: Village of Bubal (Wowol), western shore of Tulare Lake (Cook note [6]). Fr. Luis Antonio Martinez burned village of Pusas. Lively controversy with other friars over his behavior.
sources: Cook, pp. 279–286. Cook note [6]: Bubal = Wowol, NE shore of Tulare Lake.

name: Battle at Malime/Tasnil
summary: Near Santa Cruz. Inter-indigenous: Christians vs. gentiles. 4 killed.

name: Armed Encounter on Sacramento
summary: Sacramento River, ~50 miles from SF. Standoff; Spanish retreated.

name: Skirmish with Fugitives on Sacramento
summary: Sacramento delta island. Minor skirmish; 1 wounded.

name: Campaign against Muquelenis
summary: Mokelumne, eastern delta. Spanish reported success.

name: Garcia's Battle at Jayaya/Tape
summary: Beyond Santa Rita, near SJB. Spanish nearly defeated; chaotic retreat.

name: Boronda's Campaign at Tachi
summary: Tachi village, west of Tulare Lake near Lemoore (Cook note [2]: "not far west of Lemoore"). Same village Ortega raided in Nov 1815. "Arrows raining; underground defenses." Indigenous fortification.
sources: Bancroft II. Cook note [2] on Tachi location.

name: Moraga's Failed Expedition to Amajava
summary: Interior Santa Barbara. "Impassable roads, no water/pasture. Sin haber podido llegar á la ranchería de los Amajavas." Gentiles attacked others as reprisal for mission punishment.

name: Killing of Padre Quintana at Santa Cruz
summary: Neophytes at Mission Santa Cruz killed Padre Andrés Quintana after he used a wire-tipped whip on them. Lorenzo Asisara's eyewitness account (recorded 1877) is the primary source. Planned conspiracy among neophytes — lured padre out at night, strangled and mutilated him. Death concealed as natural for years.
result: Padre killed — indigenous resistance to mission abuse
sources: Lorenzo Asisara account (Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 284–293). Bancroft II.

name: Sánchez SF Campaign
summary: SF area. Solá: campaign under Sargento Sánchez "fue muy brillante." Recommended for alférez despite near-invalid status.

name: Battle of Punta de Guijarros (Lelia Byrd)
summary: FIRST NAVAL ENGAGEMENT in California. Shore battery vs. American frigate. Grapeshot exchanged; ship escaped.

name: Battle at Santa Catalina Frontera
summary: Baja California frontier. Spanish victory in two phases; chief killed. Sargento Salgado wounded — musket ball lodged.

name: English Ship at Bodega with Cannons on Land
summary: English ship anchored at Bodega with cannons on land — possible temporary fortification. Same year as Spanish Bodega colonization failure.

name: De la Cuadra Emergency Battery at Monterey
summary: Naval captain from Nootka finds NO defense at Monterey landing. Orders 4-cannon battery built on overlooking hill.

name: English Launches at Santa Cruz
summary: English launches at Santa Cruz coast; withdrew morning of Nov 28. Sargento Pedro Amador sent as reinforcement. Soldier Joaquín Mesa punished for misreporting.

name: San Diego Harbor Defense Protocol
summary: Detailed defensive orders: artillery alternates 24-hr guard; live sentinel at batería; watch bocana del Estrecho. English aware of SJC fondeadero. Pedreros recommended at SJC embarcadero.

name: American Vessel Captured at Refugio
summary: Sargento Carlos Carrillo reported 2 vessels. Alférez with 14 men captured 2 Americans + 2 Indians + 1 negro. Then 6 more Americans + 2 captains. Goleta prisionera.

name: Post-Bouchard Defense: 150 Machetes
summary: Solá + Fr. Payeras: construct 150 machetes + cartucheras — "por el corto número de espadas que tienen." Model machetes sent to missions for production. Desperate arming.

name: English Corsair Campaign — CA Response
summary: 4 English frigates (22–24 guns each) raided Gulf of California shipping. Gov. Arrillaga: "sin ningunos elementos de defensa." 50-man militia raised; battery formed at Puerto Escondido.
result: English naval dominance — Spanish desperate defense

name: Bouchard's Attack on Monterey
summary: MAJOR NAVAL RAID. Argentine privateer Bouchard: Argentina (38 guns + 2 howitzers) + Santa Rosa (26 guns), ~285 crew. Spanish: 40 defenders (25 cavalry, 4 veteran artillery, 11 militia). Nov 21: 2-hour artillery duel — J.J. Vallejo's beach battery did most damage; 5+ insurgents killed. Nov 22: 400 men landed with 4 field pieces; Estrada's 25 men overwhelmed. Solá retreated to Salinas, saving archives + 6,000 cartridges. Presidio burned Nov 26-27. Damage: ~$10,000. Only foreign capture of a California capital.
result: Only time a California presidio fell to an enemy — Monterey burned
sources: Solá, Noticia (report to viceroy). Prov. Rec. XII.164 (C-A 26). Prov. St. Pap. XX.228-30 (C-A 13). Ben. Mil. XLIX (C-A 17). 15+ Californio testimonies. Bancroft II, 227-243.

name: Bouchard's Raid on Refugio Ranch
summary: Dec 2: Bouchard landed, plundered and burned Ortega's ranch, killed cattle. Sgt. Carlos Carrillo with 30 men reached Sta Inés at 11:30 PM. Padres sent 40 armed neophytes + 35 more from SLO. Spanish ambush party captured 3 insurgents (Lt. William Taylor of Boston, Martin Romero, Mateo José Pascual).
result: Ranch destroyed; 3 insurgents captured by ambush
sources: Padre Ullibarri to Payeras (Arch. Arzob. III.ii.24-6). Bancroft II, 236-237.

name: Bouchard at San Juan Capistrano
summary: Dec 14-15: Alf. Santiago Argüello with 30 men from San Diego posted on hill. Bouchard landed, requested supplies, refused. Minor damage — straw houses burned, wine/brandy spilled. 4 insurgent deserters surrendered (John Rose, Scotch drummer; Pedro Zaldíbar; Nicolás Chavarría; Mateo José Pascual).
sources: Bancroft II, 240-241. Prov. St. Pap. XX.62; Ben. Mil. XLIX.66-72 (prisoner testimony).

name: Santa Clara Uprising Threat
summary: Fray Diego de Noboa warns: rancherías augmented, "rompimiento muy semejante al de Sonora" (uprising like Sonora). Exiled gentiles from Monterey/Santa Cruz fled with Christians. "Situación muy crítica."

name: Stockade at Mission San José
summary: Estacada ordered: 70 varas long, 60 varas wide. Soldiers must carry arms at all times; weekly arms and cartridge inspection. Pedro Amador instructions.

name: Indian Prisoners at San Diego
summary: "Indios presidiarios" maintained at $693/year at San Diego presidio. Indigenous prisoners as fiscal/military burden.

name: Neophyte Hermenegildo Condemned to Death
summary: Neophyte sentenced to capital punishment at San Diego. Extreme measure.

name: Arms Inventory ALL CA Presidios
summary: Complete "Relación del Armamento" at San Gabriel. ALL 5 presidios: 263 swords (72 short), 376 mohanas (22 short), 289 escopetas (51 short), 434 pistol pairs. SF: 32 swords (13 short), 55 mohanas, 39 escopetas (12 short).

name: Chumash Revolt — Santa Inés
summary: LARGEST INDIGENOUS UPRISING IN CALIFORNIA. Trigger: flogging of Purísima neophyte by Cpl. Cota. Feb 21 afternoon: neophytes attacked soldiers, set fire to "a large part of mission buildings." Sgt. Anastasio Carrillo arrived next day with reinforcements. Hostile Indians fled to Purísima.
result: Chumash driven off — revolt spreads to La Purísima and Santa Barbara
sources: Rafael González (Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 323-365). Bancroft II, 527-536. Ripoll, Levantamiento, MS.

name: Chumash Revolt — La Purísima Seized
summary: Same afternoon as Sta Inés. Cpl. Tapia + 4-5 soldiers defended families through night; surrendered when powder ran out. 4 white travelers killed (Sepúlveda, Sotelo + 2). 7 Indians killed. ~400 neophytes then fortified mission: palisades, loopholes in adobe, mounted old cannon + 2 swivel-guns, drilled for month. Padre Rodríguez stayed — "no disposition to molest him."
result: INDIGENOUS VICTORY — Chumash held fortified mission for 29 days
sources: Bancroft II, 528-533. Purísima Lib. Mis., MS., 11. Beebe & Senkewicz, 323-365.

name: Chumash Revolt — Santa Barbara
summary: Feb 22: Neophytes under Andrés armed themselves. Fought Capt. Guerra's troops "several hours" from behind corridor pillars with guns and arrows. Indians: 2 killed, 3 wounded (28 total dead later). Spanish: 4 wounded. Indians took property (respected church), fled to hills then Tulares. Ripoll "sat at a window at the presidio overlooking the mission, weeping."
result: Chumash strategic withdrawal to Tulares — retained autonomy for 4 months
sources: Ripoll, Levantamiento, MS. Ord, Ocurrencias, MS., 7-11. Bancroft II, 530-531.

name: Recapture of La Purísima
summary: Lt. Estrada: 109 men (16 artillery, 23 cavalry, 35 infantry, 35 Indian auxiliaries) + 4-pounder. 8 AM attack. 400 neophytes returned fire from cannon, swivels, muskets + arrows. Neophyte guns ineffective — "ignorance of gunners." Canon may have burst, killing defenders. Cavalry cut off escape. P. Rodríguez interceded. Over at 10:30 AM. Indians: 16 killed, many wounded. Spanish: 3 wounded (1 mortal). 7 sentenced to death + shot. 4 ringleaders: 10 yrs exile.
result: Mexican victory — 2.5 hour battle; 7 Chumash leaders executed
sources: Estrada report (Dept. St. Pap. I.166-9, C-A 27). Gonzalez, Experiencias, MS. Bancroft II, 532-534.

name: Estanislao's First Revolt — Sánchez Defeated
summary: ESTANISLAO — "a neophyte of more than ordinary ability, educated at Mission San José, of which establishment he was at one time alcalde." Ran away 1827-28 with CIPRIANO. By Nov 1828 PP. Durán and Viader warned Martínez of general rising. Earlier ~20-man expedition accomplished nothing. Estanislao continued "insulting challenges to the soldiers." May 5-8: Alf. Sánchez with ~40 men + damaged swivel-gun. Divided into 6 parties of 6 under Cpls. Piña, Berreyesa, Soto. 2 of Piña's men "so rash as to enter the wood" — killed. "Ammunition exhausted, men tired out, weather excessively hot." Estanislao left unconquered. Cpl. Soto and pvts. Peña and Pacheco praised for gallantry.
result: INDIGENOUS VICTORY — Sánchez's force repulsed with heavy losses (~50% casualty rate)
sources: Sánchez, Campaña contra Estanislao, 1829, MS. Durán/Viader to Martínez Nov 1828 (Arch. Arzob. V.i.68-70). Beebe & Senkewicz, 366-412. Bancroft III, 110-112.

name: Estanislao's Revolt — Vallejo's Expedition
summary: VALLEJO'S 3RD COMMAND — prior campaigns: Sierra Nevada from S. Miguel (1 killed, 15 wounded), Tulares (35 men, "slain 48 Indians, no casualties"). 107 men + 3-pounder. Set impenetrable wood on fire. Sánchez with 25 men fought 2+ hrs in burning wood. Vallejo with 37 men found "pits and ditches arranged with considerable skill" — "Indians could never have been dislodged except by the agency employed." Pursued to Arroyo Seco: "some negotiations" then Indians declared "they would die rather than surrender." Cut road through chaparral with axes; 8 wounded; ammunition ran out. Night: Indians escaped one by one. Morning: only dead bodies + 3 women. ATROCITY: Piña records 6+ captives killed. Osio: "order of no quarter given by Vallejo, infuriated auxiliaries wrought terrible carnage, pits for defences utilized as graves." Fiscal concluded: only 1 man + 1 woman killed — woman "unjustifiably by soldier Joaquín Alvarado." Martínez: "no decisive results obtained." Pardoned Oct 7, 1829.
result: Pyrrhic Mexican victory — Estanislao escaped; atrocity investigation
sources: Vallejo, Campaña, 1829. Piña, Diario, 1829. Osio, Hist. Cal., 126-38. Davis, Glimpses, 282-9. Vallejo Doc. XX.280 (prior campaigns). Bancroft III, 110-114.

name: Vallejo vs. Guapos (Sotoyomes)
summary: VALLEJO'S SONOMA FRONTIER. Cainameros (allies near Sta Rosa) attacked by Sotoyomes/"Guapos" — 22 killed, 50 wounded. Chiefs appealed to Vallejo. Apr 1: 50 soldiers + 100 Indians + Cainamero force. Salvador Vallejo commanded one division. Apr 4: Battle in Geyser region hills. Guapos "routed and driven back to their rancherías, where most of them were killed." Apr 7: back at Sonoma, zero casualties. Jun 7: TREATY with 7 tribes (Yoloytoy, Guilitoy, Ansactoy, Liguaytoy, Aclutoy, Churuptoy, Guapos). Approved by Gov. Chico. 1837: Chief Zampay of Yoloytoy revolted — captured by Solano + Salvador Vallejo; eventually became "peaceful citizen and industrious farmer."
result: Decisive Mexican victory — zero casualties; 7-tribe treaty followed
sources: Vallejo Doc. III.104-105 (orders, report). III.119,217 (treaty). Dept. St. Pap. Ben. Mil. LXXXI.25-7. Bancroft IV, 70-72.

name: Battle of Soscol — Horse Thieves
summary: 50 Indian horse-thieves crossed Sacramento, appeared at Soscol with band of tame horses, aiming to stampede Sonoma horses. 34 killed in battle with Vallejo's men; rest surrendered. Chief CUMUCHÍ shot at Sonoma for his crimes.
result: Decisive Mexican victory — 34 killed, 0 Mexican casualties

name: Capture of Zampay (Yoloytoy)
summary: Chief ZAMPAY of the Yoloytoy broke the 1836 treaty, "committing many outrages and trying to arouse the Sotoyomes again." Head chief MOTI offered to aid his capture. Combined forces of SOLANO and SALVADOR VALLEJO effected capture. Zampay held captive at Sonoma but eventually "became a peaceful citizen and industrious farmer."
sources: Vallejo, Doc., MS., iv. 250,256. Salvador Vallejo, Notas Hist., MS., 7-65. Bancroft IV, 72.

name: Sacking of Pacheco/Sánchez Ranchos
summary: Indians sacked ranchos of Pacheco and Sánchez near San Juan Bautista: killed 1 white man, "outraging several women, burning the buildings, and destroying all they could lay their hands on." Sánchez rancho also attacked March 1837 (2 Indians killed). Riflemen at Monterey demanded $2 each before pursuing.
sources: Vallejo, Doc., MS., v. 116-17, 129. Bancroft IV, 75-76.

name: Death of Yozcolo at Santa Clara
summary: Party of YOZCOLOS attacked neophytes guarding wheat-fields at Santa Clara, killing 1. Leader DIOSCULO/YOZCOLO was killed and "his head set up on a pole at the mission." This is the same Yoscolo linked to Estanislao by Davis — confirmed ex-neophyte resistance leader.
sources: St. Pap., Miss., MS., ix. 60-1. Vallejo, Doc., MS., viii. 4, 41. Bancroft IV, 76.

name: Prado Mesa Ambush on Río de Estanislao
summary: Alf. PRADO MESA on expedition against thieves on "Río de Estanislao" (Stanislaus River). Surprised by the foe. 3 citizen volunteers killed, Mesa wounded, 6 of his men wounded, many weapons lost. Desiderio Briones left behind — found 7 days later by Francisco Palomares. "This disaster caused much excitement and alarm. The Indians became bolder than ever."
result: INDIGENOUS VICTORY — Mexican force routed with heavy losses
sources: Vallejo, Doc., MS., xxv.249, viii.368-395. Bancroft IV, 76.

name: Sonoma Native Infantry Revolt
summary: Vallejo's own Indian infantry company — "about 25 selected Indians given Spanish names on the rosters, armed with muskets" — attempted revolt. "Many of the number were killed in a fight, and nine were subsequently shot." A trained indigenous military unit turned against its commanders.
sources: Vallejo, Doc., MS., viii. 192; xiv. 18; xxxiii. 56-7. Bancroft IV, 74.

name: CLEAR LAKE MASSACRE — Salvador Vallejo
summary: LARGEST SINGLE-DAY DEATH TOLL IN CALIFORNIA. Capt. SALVADOR VALLEJO: 70 Californians + 200 auxiliary Indians. 12 soldiers + 30 auxiliaries reached island of "Moth" by rafts. Night attack 8-11 PM. 170 INDIANS KILLED. Official report placed island "near Cape Mendocino" — actually Clear Lake. Bojorges: real objective was slave-raiding ("to obtain Indian laborers and servants"). Dr. Sandels: attack was "in the absence of all the fighting men." Negro deserter "shot in the back." Governor ordered investigation — result unknown. Tribes: Mottiyomi, Chiliyomi, Holiyomi, Tuliyomi, Supuyomi, + 4 more.
sources: Salvador Vallejo to M.G. Vallejo, Mar 13 (Vallejo Doc. XI.342). Report Apr 1 (XI.354). Governor investigation order Apr 26 (XI.366). Bojorges, Recuerdos, 24-37. Bancroft IV, 162-163.

name: Chaguanosos Steal 1,200 Horses from SLO
summary: CHAGUANOSOS (Shawnees from New Mexico, under Canadian chiefs, in league with trappers). Stole 1,200 horses from San Luis Obispo. "Well armed, ready for any kind of profitable adventure." Pursuers from LA saw "the foe retiring deliberately and independently" but "thought it imprudent to risk a conflict, especially when they saw that among the Chaguanosos there were more Americans than Indians."
result: Raiders escaped unchallenged — Mexicans too afraid to attack
sources: Dept. St. Pap., Angeles, MS., iv.72,88. Bancroft IV, 77.

name: Indian Attacks near San Diego
summary: Late-period indigenous military action near San Diego. Juana Machado's firsthand account describes continued frontier violence in the Mexican period.

name: Indian Attacks near Guadalupe
summary: Late frontier violence near Guadalupe (Santa Barbara County). José Luciano Espinosa and María Gracia accounts describe indigenous raids in the final years before American conquest.
sources: Espinosa & María Gracia accounts (Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 420–427).

name: Castillo de San Joaquín fires on the Saturnina (friendly fire)
summary: Earliest known shore-battery firing at San Francisco. Hermenegildo Sal's guard sighted a sail at 8 a.m. and fired a warning shot; getting no reply, manned the Cantil Blanco battery (8 cannon — the future Castillo de San Joaquín / Fort Point) intending to sink her, and fired 8 cannon-shots from 11 a.m. until 5 p.m. Pilot Juan Carrasco then identified her as the Spanish navy schooner Saturnina, carrying Bodega y Quadra's Nootka dispatches, driven in for water.
result: Friendly-fire blunder — predates the 1803 Lelia Byrd as a battery-fires-on-ship episode
sources: C-A 55 i.304–5 / scan n68–69 (Doc 135). ⚠ Check Bancroft.

name: English ships at Bodega Bay (Indian intelligence)
summary: An Indian "de las Islas" reports two ships at Puerto de la Bodega, ashore 4 days taking water and wood, hunting deer, and trading otter skins for cattle; the sailors believed English. Sal to Arrillaga. Foreign use of unguarded Bodega as the back door to SF Bay during the Nootka-crisis / Vancouver season. Related to #41 (1793 English ship at Bodega with cannons on land).
result: Coastal-defense intelligence — consecutive-year foreign presence at the bay Spain meant to occupy

name: The "Sochem" engagement
summary: After gentiles killed a Christian Indian of Mission San Miguel, an alférez + 5 soldiers seized 3 of the killers; on the return ~300 armed Indians attacked the patrol. The soldiers killed 1, wounded 2 and withdrew, but the alférez's horse was killed and the 3 captives escaped. Grajera then fortified against a feared assault on the mission.
sources: C-A 7 Doc 567 (E29), tomo xiii.507–9. ⚠ Location inferred (San Miguel de la Frontera, SD/Baja district).

name: San Luis Obispo Indian conspiracy
summary: A thwarted mission uprising: instigators Miguel and Marito used beads as recruitment currency; La Purísima gentiles were jailed and set to forced labor; the commandant downplayed it. Part of the 1794 unrest arc with the Santa Clara / SF crisis (#43); recurs in Arrillaga's "Estado de las Californias" (ringleaders sent to SF-fort forced labor).

name: The Charquín uprising at Mission San Francisco
summary: The Christian Indian Charquín rebelled at Mission San Francisco, took up arms against the mission Christians, and held ~20 mission women and children captive in the sierra; reported by Sgt. Miguel Pacheco. An early-1790s armed mission revolt led by a baptized Indian — a precursor to the Bay Area resistance cluster (#7 / #9).

name: Capture & execution of the outlaw Pomponio
summary: From Ignacio Martínez's hoja de servicios: with only a cabo + 2 soldiers he ran down and captured the cimarrón Christian José/Pomponio at the cañada de Novato after a firefight (one comrade wounded). Guilty of "innumerables robos, homicidios" (one victim a soldier), Pomponio was shot at Monterey by government order. The death sentence survives in C-A 17 Doc 389, a Consejo de Guerra condemning him for killing soldado Manuel Varela.
result: Spanish counter-banditry — Pomponio executed at Monterey
sources: C-A 18 LXVI.962–3 / n418–19 (Doc 365); C-A 17 Doc 389. ⚠ Date discrepancy — the standard account dates the execution Sept 1824; Savage's copy dates the sentence Feb 1821.

name: The Solís Revolt
summary: A convict-sergeant pronunciamiento: presidiario Sgt. Joaquín Solís and subcomisario José Mª Herrera seized Monterey against Gov. Echeandía; marched south; the SF garrison fired a salute and raised the flag to welcome them; defeated at Santa Bárbara (Pacheco's counter-revolution). Leaders deported to San Blas on the Volunteer. Prosecution built a "revolución por España" charge and named a foreign-merchant circle (Hartnell/Spence/Anderson/Stearns) on both sides.
result: Mexican-period military coup — suppressed; leaders deported

name: Post-Victoria Civil War (Ávila/Sánchez vs. Zamorano)
summary: After Gov. Victoria's overthrow, Vicente Sánchez and the convict José Antonio Ávila armed a band of presidiarios (weapons from Santa Bárbara) against Zamorano's northern faction. Ávila's party split from Sánchez past San Buenaventura and surrendered at Mission Santa Inés; dismissed under the 25 Apr 1832 amnesty. The endpoint of the Solís→anti-Victoria arc.

name: Estanislao-campaign atrocity inquiry
summary: Fiscal Ignacio Martínez investigates Indians of San José and Santa Clara killed on a punitive expedition under an alférez "de Vallejo" (⚠ likely M. G. Vallejo), including an elderly gentile woman killed by a Monterey soldier — "no le es permitido á ninguna persona atacar á los del sexo femenino." Inferred to be the aftermath of Vallejo's May–June 1829 Estanislao campaign.

name: Pursuit & killing of the fugitive Valerio
summary: Alf. Rodrigo del Pliego's hoja de servicios: caught the cimarrón Indian Valerio "herido de un balazo, de cuyas resultas murió luego," 19 Jan 1828. Almost certainly the noted Santa Bárbara-region fugitive neophyte Valerio.

name: Pacification of the Mission San Fernando Indians
summary: Del Pliego's record: with 20 men, captured the cabecillas and brought them prisoner to Santa Bárbara (21 Jul 1828).

name: San Diego Indian-uprising conspiracy
summary: A planned assault on the SD presidio — "acabar con los blancos p[ar]a ser independientes, y gobernarse ellos mismos" — extending to the missions up to San Gabriel. Ringleaders Molina and Tesochi got 2 years (attenuated for "ignorancia"); the authorities conceded "la probabilidad de los malos tratamientos" as motive. Figueroa confirmed 22 Nov 1833.

name: New Mexico horse-thief expedition into the Tulares
summary: After New Mexico "aventureros/ladrones" (allied with Tulares gentiles) drove off ~1,000 head (incl. 108 mules from Mission San Miguel; SLO stock recovered near Arroyo Grande), Gov. Figueroa ordered a 15-cavalry punitive expedition under Alf. Dámaso Rodríguez "á perseguir y destruir" the band. An early Old-Spanish-Trail horse-raiding record.

name: Felipe Briones killed at Cerro de Bolbones
summary: Felipe Briones killed trying to recover horses from Indians near the Cerro de Bolbones (Mt Diablo area).
sources: Vallejo, Doc., MS., ix. 20. Bancroft IV, 76 n70.

name: Martínez defeats Indians near Mt Diablo
summary: Lt. Martínez defeated Indians near Mt Diablo, "Marsh with other Americans rendering valuable service." Report dated Feb 7.

name: Sutter's Indian executions
summary: Indians surrendered at a lake 30 mi south of the fort; "14 were put to death." Vallejo says a force of Californians sent to the valley multiple times rescued captives and took ~80 prisoners.

name: Cochrane squadron raid on Mission Todos Santos
summary: Ships of "la Escuadra del Lord Cochrane" entered Cabo de Cortés and attacked Mission Todos Santos at night; the inhabitants defended themselves, killing the lieutenant + 4 men, mortally wounding 3, and capturing the rest — a 13-man landing party. Gov. Solá ordered church silver withdrawn from the coast.
sources: C-A 54 ii.456+ / scan n211–212 (Doc II-51). ⚠ Reconcile year (Cochrane's Pacific ops ~1822).

name: Ambush near San Vicente / Santa Catalina
summary: An escort transporting 5 captured Indians was ambushed: 2 soldiers killed — Juan Camacho + one other — beaten on the head with stones, their muskets found loaded but broken; the prisoners escaped; the bodies buried at San Vicente. Distinct from #37 (the 1804 Santa Catalina Spanish victory).
sources: C-A 54 iv.346–9 / scan n468–470. ⚠ Pin the exact year.

name: Spanish Yuma Punitive Campaign
summary: Neve and Capt. José Antonio Romeu (108 men) march down the Colorado; Romeu with 40 men and 12 Indian allies (Jalchedunes/Cocomaricopas) routs the Yuma cavalry ('crecida mortandad'), 4 soldiers wounded; Fages escorts the rescued captives. An abortive plan to re-fortify the crossing follows. Neve's 'Antecedentes de una mortandad' names the revolt's causes: gifts withheld from Palma, women seized, mules destroying the mesquite.
result: Spanish reprisal after the 1781 Yuma victory

name: Tule balsa shipwreck at the Farallones
summary: Mission San Francisco Indians navigated by sea in tule balsas to evangelize gentiles on the opposite shore; currents carried one balsa onto the Farallones, where 2 of the 4 Indians drowned. Not a military engagement — an indigenous open-water navigation datum at the Bay mouth.

name: Horse-theft raid & punitive expedition (140-head caballada)
summary: 'Indios malhechores' drove off a caballada of 140 head from the national rancho; Gov. Argüello sent Alf. Santiago Argüello with 34 troops to punish the raiders and the rancherías that harbor them ('por cortar de raiz los daños'). In a related San Diego–frontier action, 14 of 15 neophyte auxiliaries who fought were killed, and ~40 raiders later burned a San Diego rancho.
notes: Rancherías not located in the abstract — pin placed in the Monterey-district interior, conjectural. Verbatim re-read flagged in the register.

name: Surrender of the Spanish squadron at Monterey
summary: The Spanish ship-of-the-line Asia and brig Constante — Spain's last Pacific force after the loss of Peru — put into Monterey and capitulated to the Mexican Nation, their crews swearing 'el solemne juramento de sujetarse á las leyes de los Estados Unidos Mejicanos.' Argüello tapped the Asia's powder to arm the near-empty San Francisco and Monterey batteries; pilot Juan Malarín carried the acta de Capitulación to Mexico City.
sources: Dep. Rec. (C-A 46), Docs 49–52, 102; cf. Bancroft, Hist. Cal., III

name: The anti-Victoria revolt — the Cahuenga encounter
summary: California's first successful gubernatorial overthrow. The Plan of San Diego (29 Nov 1831) pronounced against Gov. Manuel Victoria 'por recobrar la representación popular'; Victoria was wounded at the Cahuenga encounter ('estado moribundo') and deported on the Pocahontas. The interregnum then splintered — the Diputación installed Pío Pico, Los Angeles proclaimed Echeandía, Zamorano rose in the north — 'casi inevitable el derramamiento de sangre.'
notes: Pin at Cahuenga Pass, the revolt's one armed encounter; the movement spanned San Diego to Monterey. Verbatim re-read flagged.

name: The anti-Micheltorena revolt — Cahuenga/Providencia
summary: The last Californio overthrow of a Mexican-appointed governor. The Asamblea Departamental 'desconoce la autoridad… de M. Micheltorena,' charging his 'relaciones secretas con el extranjero Sutter' (Sutter marched for Micheltorena; M.G. Vallejo corresponded against him). Micheltorena and his cholo battalion were defeated and expelled after the Battle of Cahuenga/Providencia, installing the native-born Pío Pico as California's last Mexican governor.
notes: The Sutter alliance prefigures the American takeover 16 months later. Verbatim re-read flagged (incl. the deposition-session date).

name: Ambush of the San Diego pursuit party (Dominican frontier)
summary: A San Diego troop detachment pursuing Dominican-mission cimarrones (runaway neophytes) was ambushed on the Baja frontier; the action left 15 Indians dead, including a leader from Mission San Miguel — a frontier engagement in the same season as the Chumash Revolt further north.
result: Indigenous tactical victory over a presidial party
notes: Ambush site not located in the abstract — pin placed on the Dominican-mission frontier, conjectural.

name: Gentile attack on mission neophytes — '21 neophytes killed'
summary: A report of 21 neophytes killed by gentiles on the interior frontier of the San Diego district — evidence for the raiding-economy pressure on mission populations in the 1820s, and for inter-indigenous violence between mission and independent communities.
notes: Location not given in the abstract — conjectural pin in the SD interior.

name: Martínez's 'campaña en los Ríos'
summary: Ignacio Martínez's 1827 campaign into the Delta rivers against the runaway and rebel Indians of Missions San José and Santa Clara — 2 soldiers killed. The precursor operation to the 1829 Estanislao campaigns, in the same theater.
sources: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53), Doc 8, tomo pp. 21–23

name: Vallejo's & Sánchez's punitive horse-raid expeditions
summary: Stock-raid reprisal operations by Vallejo and Sánchez in the Monterey district interior, mounted while the Monterey troops were destitute (the pay-mutiny backdrop, six months before the Solís revolt).
notes: Routes not located in the abstract — conjectural pin in the Monterey interior.

name: The great 1840 horse-raid campaign ('agua de Ramón')
summary: A multi-week mounted campaign, not a single skirmish. Chaguanosos drove off three mare-herds from Mission San Gabriel (14 May); the LA vecino-militia mobilized (13→36 men, 4 muskets, 19 lances); Ignacio Palomares's ~23-man party was routed; relief columns held the Puerta del Cajón and recovered the herds at the 'agua de Ramón' surprise. Leandri's report: the rearguard was '20 hombres Ciudadanos de los Estados Unidos; pero tenían á más otra gente,' driving 1,000+ head, ~1,500 driven to death in flight.
result: Raiders routed one militia party; herds recovered at agua de Ramón
notes: Pin at Cajon Pass, the campaign's hinge; 'agua de Ramón' itself is unlocated (secondary lit puts the fight at Resting Springs — the partes' 'Ramón' is a datum to weigh vs Bancroft IV:77, not to harmonize).

name: Plaza firefight (Alvarado civil war)
summary: Californio-vs-Californio action of the 1838 civil war (the Alvarado–Carrillo contest for the governorship): per José Castro's abstracted report, a 160-man force garrisoning 'la plaza' abandoned it by night after a 2-day continuous firefight with only 1 dead, pursued by cavalry; two bronze cannon at the Castillo were transferred to José Estrada.
result: Garrison abandoned 'la plaza' after a 2-day firefight
sources: Dep. St. Pap., Monterey (C-A 43), Doc 4 (n420, tomo 41)
notes: 'La plaza' is not named on the leaf — probably Santa Bárbara or a southern plaza. Conjectural pin; transcribe the parte before locating or quoting.

name: Ibarra's punitive battle at Santa Isabel ('veinte pares de orejas')
summary: A punitive action against ~100 or more hostile Indians, routed 4 leguas to San Felipe with Kumeyaay/Cahuilla auxiliaries; 28 killed, '20 pares de orejas' cut from the dead as proof, plus a prisoner summarily executed.
sources: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Doc 914 (Expanded Entry 26)
notes: Abstract — transcribe the parte before quoting the orejas line.

name: José Castro's 120-man northern expedition 'contra los bárbaros'
summary: A late-Mexican state campaign against interior raiders — 120 men under Castro 'contra los bárbaros que invaden los puntos del norte.'
result: State campaign (outcome not recorded in the abstract)
sources: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Doc 1228
notes: 'Los puntos del norte' unlocated — conjectural pin on the northern frontier.

name: The Gavilán (Hawk's Peak) standoff
summary: Frémont fortifies Gavilán Peak and raises the US flag; Castro musters the militia at San Juan; Frémont withdraws north by night without an engagement — the armed face-off that preceded the conquest by three months, with the prefecture's intelligence file (the Guerrero/neophyte-Antonio reports on the '200 armed foreigners').
result: Standoff — Frémont withdrew without an engagement
sources: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Docs 361–363 (Expanded Entry 22)

name: The Rancho San Francisco killing of 18 'indios ladrones'
summary: Twenty-one armed Indians were disarmed 'mañosamente,' revolted with stones and knives, and were shot — 18 dead, 2 wounded, 1 escaped: the anti-raiding apparatus at its most lethal, weeks before the conquest.
sources: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Doc 1219 (n412)
notes: District-court abstract — verify vs leaf before quoting.

name: San Pedro Mártir Indian revolt (Dominican frontier)
summary: Mission Indians revolt ('el alboroto'); the officer blamed for causing it is imprisoned at San Vicente on Borica's approval; rebels are seized and prosecuted via sumaria (Bernal spends 24 days at the mission); the investigation runs into 1797 (declarations of the indio José Manuel, later freed); the mission's interpreter is killed by mid-1797.
result: Revolt suppressed judicially; the blamed officer imprisoned
notes: A revolt + judicial-military response arc, not a field battle.

name: Río Colorado engagement ('atacar la fuerza con la fuerza')
summary: The commander, met with insults from 'una colonia numerosa' of Indians on all sides in bad terrain, 'se vió precisado á atacar la fuerza con la fuerza.' Losses: Spanish, a mule and 7–8 horses; Indian, 7 gandules killed. Reported to the Viceroy as proof the Sonora communication could not be secured without real garrisons; the Viceroy later reviewed a printed account of the engagement.
result: Spanish column fought through — 7 Indians killed
sources: Prov. Rec. (C-A 24), Doc 313 (n270, Sav 271, tomo p. 697)
notes: Site unlocated — conjectural pin near the lower Colorado. Abstract — transcribe before quoting.

name: Punitive battle near San Juan Bautista — death of captain 'Talholostl'[?]
summary: Gentiles 'de la otra banda' killed 5 Indians and 2 women of the mission rancherías; Borica sent Sgt. Macario Castro with 10 cuera soldiers, 8 Christian Indians, and 24 aggrieved gentiles; the column caught the malefactors, killed the enemy captain 'Talholostl'[?], recovered 2 women, and captured 2 — a mixed Spanish–Native punitive force whose gentile allies were the majority.
result: Punitive column killed the enemy captain
sources: Prov. Rec. (C-A 24), Sav 430 (probe n431) — Borica → Viceroy, 'Pelea con Indios'
notes: The indigenous name is uncertain on the leaf — verbatim transcription needed.

name: The Jacum battle — capitanejo Charagui defeats the San Diego column
summary: Twenty-five soldados and 50 gentiles auxiliares struck Jacum; 'el capitanejo Charagui… cargó con toda su gente y rodeando nuestras tropas nos las hicieron retirar, hiriéndonos á 9 soldados y 17 indios de los auxiliares… quedó el campo pr los enemigos.' The raiders had already run off the Misión San Diego caballada. Embedded in Zamorano's extermination-rhetoric letter; aftermath, Alvarado ordered all aid to Capt. Portilla for the pursuit (Aug 1837).
result: Indigenous victory — the field held by Charagui's force
sources: Dep. St. Pap., Angeles (C-A 37), Doc 188 (n87–n90, Sav 86–89, orig. pp. 420–424); aftermath Doc 193
notes: Charagui's people are named only as gentiles of the Jacum region in the abstract.

name: Mission San Diego de Alcalá
summary: 1769. First mission.

name: Presidio of San Diego
summary: 1769. Punta de Guijarros battery.

name: Mission Santa Bárbara
summary: 1786. Presidio 1782.

name: Presidio of Monterey
summary: 1770. Capital.

name: Mission San Rafael
summary: 1817. Site selected by Moraga.

name: Fort Ross (Russian)
summary: 1812. Russian-American Company.

name: Mission San Juan Bautista
summary: 1797. Moraga 1806 departure; Viader 1810 Aug return.

name: Mission San Fernando
summary: 1797. Moraga 1806 arrival.

name: Mission San José
summary: 1797. Viader/Moraga 1810 departure point.

name: Mission Santa Clara
summary: 1777. Viader 1810 Oct return.

name: Mission San Miguel
summary: 1797. Moraga 1807 stop.

name: Monterey Presidio
summary: Moraga 1807 departure.

name: Mission San Rafael
summary: 1817. First establishment north of SF Bay. Site selected by Moraga. Founded 83 days after Hagemeister-Pomo treaty.

name: Fort Ross
summary: 1812. Russian-American Company. Kuskov. 95 Russians + ~80 Aleut hunters. Moraga visited 1812, 1813, 1814.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: 1776. Founded by José Joaquín Moraga (Gabriel's father). Gabriel served here as Alférez 1806–1818.

name: Monterey
summary: Departure. 25 cavalry under Alférez Gabriel Moraga. Sgt. Miguel Espinosa. 8 horses each.

name: Mission San Miguel
summary: 'We went from Monterey to San Miguel Mission.'

name: Buena Vista Lake
summary: 'From there to the Laguna we called Buena Vista in one day and a half.' Went after runaway neophytes — 'did not get any.'
sources: Cook note 46: 'This expedition of 1807 is otherwise unrecorded.'

name: Mountains — Salinas de Cortéz
summary: 'We went away into the Snowy Mountains.' Indians stole half the horses and killed two men. 'Portezuelo' with great quantities of nitre.
sources: Cook note 47: possibly entrance to Gorge of Kern R., E of Bakersfield

name: San Joaquin River (crossings)
summary: 'We crossed the San Joaquin River several times and everywhere there was Indians.'

name: Mission San José
summary: 'The Captain made up his mind to go back by the way of San Jose Mission where we arrived in good order.'

name: Mission San José
summary: Aug 15, 1810. Departed 5:30pm with Lt. Moraga, Cadet Estrada, 1 corporal, 3 soldiers, 4 neophytes.

name: Valley of San José — Pool
summary: Aug 15. 6 leagues N. 'Good water. So near and so well known to everyone.'

name: Walnut Creek
summary: Aug 16 (lunch). 6 leagues N. 'Good water but runs in very small amount.' Killed 2 bears and a deer.
sources: Cook note 1: route via Sunol, Dublin, Walnut Creek

name: End of Walnut Creek — Estuaries
summary: Aug 16. 6 more leagues N. 'Beginning of some estuaries on the NE side of a fine plain.' Big walnut trees.

name: San Joaquin R. / Tulpunes — Antioch
summary: Aug 18. 7 leagues E. 'Quarter-league wide, apparently very deep, reached by tides.' Large oak forest. Tulpunes not seen.
sources: Cook note 2: at or near Antioch. Julpunes oak forest to Brentwood.

name: Oak Forest — Oakley
summary: Aug 18. 2 more leagues E. Good country, no water except the river.

name: Pescadero / Cholvones — Bethany
summary: Aug 19. 10 leagues SE. Lake in oak grove. Interpreter met heathen Guanats. 'Christian fugitives from San Jose on opposite shore.'

name: Tomchom Village
summary: Aug 20. 3 leagues SSE. 'No wild Indian showed himself.'

name: Cuyens Village
summary: Aug 20. 2½ leagues further. Chief Cuyens. 15 Indians brought fish. 4 stayed the night.
sources: Cook note 4: left bank, 3 mi above highway bridge

name: Maijem Village
summary: Aug 21. 2½–3 leagues. Dry stream bed. Indians came 'with great trepidation' then disappeared.
sources: Cook note 5: ~2 mi NNE of Vernalis

name: Opposite Bozenats Village
summary: Aug 21. 2 leagues. Indians on far bank 'refused to come near, yelled with much vigor.'
sources: Cook note 6: right bank, E of Vernalis

name: SKIRMISH — Tationes / Apaglamenes
summary: Aug 22. 2 leagues + 1 league. 30 armed heathen on opposite bank. Then Tationes village. Apaglamenes and Tationes attacked — 'painted, bedecked with feathers.' 12 shots fired. One Indian hit.
sources: Cook note 7: ~2 mi SE of Grayson

name: Lakes near Willow Grove
summary: Aug 22 afternoon. 3 leagues. 'Neither firewood nor good land.'
sources: Cook note 8: probably E of Patterson

name: Creek — Orestimba
summary: Aug 23. 3½ leagues. 'Great heat forced us to jump in for a swim.'
sources: Cook note 9: Orestimba Cr., E of Crows Landing

name: Pond — NE of Gustine
summary: Aug 23. 4 leagues. 'No wood or brush to cook supper or even make chocolate.'
sources: Cook note 10: 3–4 mi NE of Gustine. Southernmost point.

name: San Luis Gonzaga
summary: Aug 24. Turned back W! 10 leagues. Explored — 'no more water than a few pools.' Not suitable for mission.

name: Ausaymas Creek
summary: Aug 26. SW over mountains. 'Caught forty fish including six trout.' Already the plain of San Juan Bautista.

name: Mission San Juan Bautista
summary: Aug 27. 5 leagues SW. 'With a good breakfast we brought the expedition to an end.' Signed Fr. José Viader, Aug 28, 1810.

name: Mission San José
summary: Oct 19, 1810. Left ~2pm with Lt. Moraga, 23 soldiers, ~50 armed Christian Indians.

name: Valley of San José
summary: Oct 19. 6 leagues NE. Willow grove with good water.

name: Pescadero / Cholvones — Bethany
summary: Oct 20. 9 leagues ENE. 'Arrived very late so as not to be seen or heard. Placed our people in position to attack a dance.'
sources: Cook note 11: Mission San José to Bethany = ~32 mi

name: Bolbones Villages — Old River
summary: Oct 21. 'Before dawn we assaulted a village.' Captured 15 San Jose Christians, 18 heathen men, 51 women. Women released — 'went away very happy.' Two prisoners escaped.
sources: Cook note 12: two villages on Old River / Union I.

name: Jusmites
summary: Oct 22. 2½ leagues SE along river. 'Oak groves, willow thickets, ponds.'

name: Tugites
summary: Oct 22. 2½ more leagues. 'All the country has wood, water in the river and lakes, but the land is low, flooded.'

name: Cuyens Village
summary: Oct 23. Met friendly Cuyens Indians. 'Bearing as a gift three very big, red, salmon.'

name: Mayem Village
summary: Oct 23. 4 leagues total. 'Approached us with much fear and a tendency to prevaricate.' Deny harboring Christians. Lt. threatens flogging.
sources: Cook note 14: Mayem 9 leagues from Pescadero

name: Ford Opposite Taualames
summary: Oct 23 afternoon. 2 leagues. 'Ford across the river — very bad, wide and deep.' Crossed without incident. Sent boy to village.

name: Toward Tuolumne — Blocked
summary: Oct 24. Scouted N toward Tuolumne (Dolores), 2–3 leagues away. 'On account of so many sloughs, swamps, and ponds we turned back.'

name: Past Apelamenes & Tatives
summary: Oct 24 afternoon. 6 leagues SSE. 'Passed in front of the Apelamenes and Tatives Indians, who had fought us on the former occasion.'

name: Merced River
summary: Oct 24. Reached Merced R. 'Almost swimming the horses.' Much wood — oak, cypress, cottonwood.

name: Crossed San Joaquin
summary: Oct 25. SW. 'Crossed several other swampy sloughs. Southward no more trees, only tules.'

name: 'Battle / Shaving Place'
summary: Oct 25. 2½ leagues downstream. 'At this point now, as previously, the soldiers shave themselves.'

name: Orestimba Creek — Apalamenes
summary: Oct 25. 1 league. 'Allies of the Tatives when they fought us.' Creek dry. 'Least bad spot — only firewood, river water, and good fish.'

name: Opposite Taualames (return)
summary: Oct 25. 2 leagues. 'Refused to come out, saying they were afraid. They guessed right for they would have been taken captive.'

name: Del Puerto Creek — Corpus Christi
summary: Oct 25. 3 leagues W. 'Spent the night without water for the horses. Had to dig a well in the sand.'

name: El Toro
summary: Oct 26. 6 leagues W. Mountains, bad trails. Lunch stop.

name: San Guillermo
summary: Oct 26. 6 more leagues W. 'Extremely bad trail.' Pools of good water.

name: Old Village of Patenos
summary: Oct 27. 6 leagues W. Lunch stop.
sources: Cook note 16: through hills NE of Mt. Hamilton

name: Mission Santa Clara
summary: Oct 27. 5 leagues NW. 'In good condition, thanks be to God.' Signed Fr. José Viader, Oct 28, 1810.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: May 1810. Governor Arrillaga orders Moraga to pursue the Suisun, who have killed 16 Christians over 3 years. Moraga departs with 17 men.

name: Overland March — East Bay Hills
summary: Route overland from the Presidio, skirting the south shore of the Bay toward the Strait. Exact route unknown but likely through modern Oakland/Richmond hills.
sources: No diary survives for this expedition.

name: Carquinez Strait Crossing
summary: Troops had no boats. Found a single small vessel. 'With great sacrifice, the Ensign embarked his troops and horses, making several crossings.' Multiple trips across the narrows.
sources: Arrillaga report. Vallejo calls it the 'Karkin-aku pass.'

name: Skirmish on Suisun Creek
summary: Initial engagement. After crossing the strait, troops fought Suisun warriors along the banks of a creek. Vallejo (via Sánchez): the Suisun chief drew the Spanish inland toward the main body of warriors. Sgt. Francisco Soto left as rear guard with horses.
sources: Vallejo, Recuerdos Históricos, i. 131–5. Vallejo attributes to Sánchez (1817) but likely describes 1810.

name: Battle at Malaca's Ranchería
summary: Main engagement, May 22, 1810. Captured 18 gentiles (released — too wounded to transport). Survivors retreated into 3 tule huts. 2 corporals (Herrera, Soto) and 2 soldiers (Briones, Zúñiga) wounded. After 2 huts stormed, 3rd set on fire. Defenders burned alive rather than surrender. ~120 Suisun fighters. Chief Malaca's speech: 'It is better to die free than to live as slaves, despised by our Puy.'
sources: Bancroft II, p. 91 fn. 24. Arrillaga: Prov. Rec. ix. 122–3. Royal Order: Prov. St. Pap. xix. 314. Vallejo: MS. i. 131–5.

name: Return Crossing — Carquinez Strait
summary: Vallejo: 'At dawn they tried to ford a creek and came back over the Carquines Strait.' Return to south shore after the battle.

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Moraga returns to the Presidio. Arrillaga reports to the Viceroy on June 20, 1810. Royal Order arrives Nov. 12, 1811: Moraga promoted to brevet Lieutenant of Cavalry.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: October 3, 1810. Governor orders Moraga to investigate Russian fur-hunting at Bodega Bay. 10-day reconnaissance.
sources: Langelier & Rosen, fn. 52: 'Gabriel Moraga's Diary of his Expedition to the Port of Bodega, Oct. 3, 1810, Prov. St. Pap., XII, 276–79.' Diary survives in the Archives of California.

name: Bay Crossing — to Marin
summary: Crossed the bay from the Presidio to the Marin shore. Method uncertain — possibly native balsas or a borrowed vessel.
sources: SF Presidio had limited boat access. See boat-problem article.

name: San Rafael Area
summary: Passed through future site of Mission San Rafael. Bancroft: 'The site was probably selected on the advice of Moraga, who had several times passed it on his way to and from Bodega.'

name: Novato / Olompali Area
summary: Route north through Marin. Approximate — following the natural corridor through the Novato gap toward Petaluma Creek and the coast.
sources: Exact route not specified in diary abstract.

name: Petaluma Creek / Estuary
summary: Likely crossed or followed Petaluma Creek toward the coast. Natural route from interior Marin to Bodega Bay.

name: Bodega Bay
summary: Arrived at Bodega Bay. Found an American ship and Alaskan Indians (Aleut hunters working for the Russian-American Company). 10-day reconnaissance of the area. Wrote a diary of the expedition.
sources: Langelier & Rosen: 'finds an American ship and Alaskan Indians.' Diary: Prov. St. Pap., XII, 276–79.

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Returns after 10 days. Report confirms Russian presence and fur-hunting operations at Bodega.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: July 1812. Argüello sends Moraga with 4 men to investigate construction of a small Russian fort on the Sonoma coast. Fort Ross had been dedicated Sept. 10, 1812 — but Kuskov had been building since spring.

name: Bay Crossing — to Marin
summary: Crossed the bay to the Marin shore.

name: San Rafael Area
summary: Through future Mission San Rafael site. Moraga's repeated passage through here informed his later recommendation of the site.

name: Petaluma Creek Area
summary: North through Marin toward the coast. Approximate route.

name: Bodega Bay
summary: Passed through or near Bodega Bay en route to Fort Ross.

name: Fort Ross
summary: Moraga identifies the Russian settlement: a brigantine-anchored base with a 150-yard-square stockade and cannon. Notes the Russians' friendly manner. Fort Ross had been dedicated by Kuskov with 95 Russians and ~80 Aleut hunters.
sources: Langelier & Rosen: '150-yard-square stockade and cannon; notes the Russians' friendly manner.'

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Returns to SF. Report filed with Argüello.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: January 1813. Moraga drives 20 cattle and 3 horses to Fort Ross — practical diplomacy. Returns again in 1814 with Cadet Gervasio Argüello.

name: Bay Crossing — to Marin
summary: Crossed bay to Marin shore with livestock.

name: San Rafael Area
summary: Through future mission site, driving cattle north.

name: Bodega Bay Area
summary: Through Bodega country toward Fort Ross.

name: Fort Ross
summary: Jan. 1813: Delivered 20 cattle, 3 horses as goodwill gesture. 1814 return: accompanied by Cadet Gervasio Argüello. Wrote a report describing 40 gente de razón and many Kodiak Indians, cannon, glass windows in the commandant's house. Also carried official demands for Russian withdrawal.
sources: Langelier & Rosen: '40 gente de razón and many Kodiak Indians, cannon guarding the place, and glass windows in the commandant's house.'

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Returns to SF Presidio.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: c. 1817. Moraga reconnoiters the site north of the Bay for a new establishment. He had passed through this area multiple times on trips to Bodega and Fort Ross since 1810.
sources: Bancroft II, p. 330: 'The site was probably selected on the advice of Moraga.'

name: Bay Crossing — to Marin
summary: Crossed the bay to the Marin shore. SF Presidio had no proper boat from Feb. 1816 to Nov. 1818 — crossing method uncertain. Possibly native tule balsas.
sources: See boat-problem article. Vallejo mentions 'a small boat belonging to the San Francisco presidio.'

name: Mission San Rafael Site
summary: Moraga selects this site — 5 leagues north of the Presidio, on the other side of the Bay. Warmer climate, near homeland of many SF neophytes. Mission founded Dec. 14, 1817 under Fr. Luis Gil y Taboada, escorted by Cpl. José Herrera (wounded at Suisun 1810, promoted to Sgt.). Vallejo says the Suisun victory led directly to the decision to found the mission.
sources: Solá letter: Prov. Rec. ix. 583–604 (C-A 25). Founded 83 days after Hagemeister-Pomo treaty.

name: Return to Presidio
summary: Returns to the Presidio with his recommendation. Governor Solá approves the plan in consultation with Prefect Sarría.

name: Yurok
summary: Lower Klamath River and the adjacent coast.

summary: The San Diego country and northern Baja California, on both sides of the later border. From the 1775 destruction of Mission San Diego to the Jacum battle of 1837, the most persistent military resistance on the southern frontier. Yuman family.
sources: After A. L. Kroeber, Handbook of the Indians of California (1925), and standard references; homeland centers approximate.

name: Cahuilla
summary: The desert and mountain country from San Gorgonio Pass to the Salton sink; appear in the frontier records as both auxiliaries and raiders' targets.
sources: After A. L. Kroeber, Handbook of the Indians of California (1925), and standard references; homeland centers approximate.
notes: The circle marks an approximate homeland center, not a boundary. Territories interlocked, shifted, and are still contested; for living communities' own maps, see each nation's tribal government.

sources: After A. L. Kroeber, Handbook of the Indians of California (1925), and standard references; homeland centers approximate.

sources: After A. L. Kroeber, Handbook of the Indians of California (1925), and standard references; homeland centers approximate.
