sources: After A. L. Kroeber, Handbook of the Indians of California (1925), and standard references; homeland centers approximate.
notes: The circle marks an approximate homeland center, not a boundary. Territories interlocked, shifted, and are still contested; for living communities' own maps, see each nation's tribal government.

name: Presidio of San Francisco
summary: The northernmost presidio, founded 1776, responsible for the whole contested frontier facing Bodega and Ross. Its company ran about 30 to 56 men for most of the Spanish period and never surged to meet the Russian arrival in 1812.
result: Roughly 30 to 70 men guarding the Golden Gate across five decades
sources: Presidio garrison & artillery register (vault), compiled from C-A 10, 16, 21, 54

name: Castillo de San Joaquín (Fort Point, San Francisco)
summary: The battery at the Golden Gate narrows, blessed December 1794. Built for 6,491 pesos, partly with mission mule labor. The register's headline series: the fort's serviceable guns never exceeded a handful, and a single Russian outpost at Ross reported more mounted cannon than the whole San Francisco defense.
result: At its documented best (1802): about 5 mounted serviceable guns
sources: Presidio garrison & artillery register (vault); build cost C-A 24 Docs 62, 70
notes: The 1793 (2 vs 6) and 1823 (2 vs 3) figures each differ between Savage copies of the same report; both discrepancies are preserved rather than harmonized.

name: Batería de Yerba Buena (San Francisco)
summary: The cove battery east of the presidio, garrisoned in the 1790s by a Catalan Volunteers detail relieved every 8 days. Ordered recomposed with adobe, earth, and fascine in 1799; abandoned by 1800 per the intelligence layer of the borderlands map.

name: Presidio of Monterey
summary: The capital's presidio and the largest company in the province, with the Real Cuerpo de Artillería headquartered here. Even so, in 1809 its ~85 men were dispersed as mission escorts and herd guards, with only 4 men on the battery.
result: The largest single garrison, comparable to Ross's combined personnel, 100+ miles from the contested ground

name: Presidio of Santa Bárbara
summary: Founded 1782 with about 58 men and holding near that strength for decades. In 1797 its artillery inventory recorded 118 cartridges and no artilleryman.

name: Presidio of San Diego
summary: The first presidio, founded 1769. In September 1783 the whole company held 72 cartridges. Its guns were dismounted in 1793 with nobody able to operate them.

name: Fort Guijarros (Punta de Guijarros, San Diego)
summary: The harbor battery on Ballast Point, formally the Castillo de San Joaquín de Punta de Guijarros. The province had two forts of that name; this is the San Diego one. In 1798 it mounted 7 six-pounders of which 2 were serviceable; the 1820 census found the bronze guns vent-worn and the carriages rotted.

name: Presidio of Loreto (Baja California)
summary: The old Baja capital, included for context: its company (47 to 85 men plus a small marine department) was comparable to any Alta California presidio.

name: Colony Ross (Russian benchmark)
summary: The measuring stick. A single Russian-American Company outpost, founded September 1812 with about 95 Russians and 80 Alaska Native hunters. A 1813 Spanish intelligence report credited it with about 13 mounted cannon; Moraga's 1814 eyewitness reconnaissance counted 8 cannon in two corner towers with embrasures for far more. Either figure matches or exceeds the serviceable artillery of the entire San Francisco defense in the same decade.
result: One Russian fort ≈ the principal Spanish fort, in guns and in men
notes: Both the Ross figure (a Spanish estimate) and the SF figure (a Spanish self-inventory) are Spanish-sourced, so the comparison is internally consistent. The defensible claim is order-of-magnitude parity, not a precise gun-for-gun ratio.

name: Mission San Buenaventura (armed)
summary: Armed against the coastal threat: 2 four-pounders in the 1798 census, 3-pounders by 1805.

name: Mission Santa Bárbara (armed)
summary: One 2-pounder in the 1798 census of the armed Channel missions.

name: Mission La Purísima (armed)
summary: One 2-pounder in 1798, still armed in the 1803 and 1805 censuses.

name: Mission Santa Inés (armed)
summary: 3-pounders in the 1805 census of armed Channel missions.

name: Mission San Fernando (armed)
summary: 2-pounders in the 1805 census of armed missions.

name: Mission San Juan Bautista (armed, post-Bouchard)
summary: Up to 3 cannons emplaced in 1819 'para impedir el desembarco de algunos invasores', part of the scramble after Bouchard burned Monterey.

name: Pueblo San José (pedrero)
summary: A pedrero (swivel gun) posted at the pueblo in the 1798 ordnance census.

name: Mission San Luis Obispo (pedrero)
summary: A pedrero listed at 'Mission San Luis' in the 1798 Monterey-district ordnance census.
notes: The 1798 census says 'Mission San Luis' without specifying Obispo or Rey; Obispo is the Monterey-district reading. Flagged.

name: Mission Santa Ynez
summary: Founded 1804. Zalvidea departure point.

name: Mission San Gabriel
summary: Founded 1771. Zalvidea arrival.

name: Mission San Juan Bautista
summary: Founded 1797. Moraga departure point.

name: Mission San Fernando
summary: Founded 1797. Moraga arrival.

name: Jonatas
summary: At Las Olivas

name: Saca
summary: On Alamo Pintado Cr.

name: Olomosong
summary: On Sisquoc R.

name: Geep
summary: South slope Sierra Madre

name: Talhulimit
summary: 2 leagues east of Geep

name: Lisahua
summary: Salisbury Canyon

name: Sgene
summary: Lower Cuyama Valley

name: Malapoa
summary: Bitterwater/Santiago Cr.; Tlamini

name: Sispetis (Pohatan Tinliu)
summary: SE corner Buena Vista Lake

name: Tupal
summary: Tejon Ranch, El Paso Cr.

name: Tacui
summary: Tecuya Creek

name: Yagueleme (Woilo)
summary: Kern R. / Kern Lake

name: Tashlpj
summary: Pastoria / Grapevine Cr.

name: Taulames (Jahulamne)
summary: Between Tuolumne & Stanislaus; deserted by 1806

name: Pizcache
summary: On the San Joaquin, near Le Grand

name: Pitkachi
summary: South side San Joaquin; Kohou near Herndon

name: Telame (Telamni)
summary: Kaweah delta; 600 souls; 22 baptized Oct. 1806

name: Mission Santa Ynez
summary: Departure, July 20, 1806.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions

name: Jonatas — Las Olivas
summary: First stop north of mission, at Las Olivas (present-day Los Olivos area).
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 1)

name: Saca — Alamo Pintado Creek
summary: Village of Saca on Alamo Pintado Creek.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 1)

name: Olomosong — Sisquoc River
summary: Probably on the Sisquoc R. near the 120th meridian.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 1: 'After 4 leagues further travel')

name: Geep — South Slope, Sierra Madre
summary: Apparently on the south slope of the Sierra Madre range. After climbing a mountain they came out onto the Cuyama Valley plains. T 10 N, R 28 W.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 1)

name: Talhulimit
summary: Two leagues east of Geep, in the Cuyama Valley.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 1)

name: Lisahua — Salisbury Canyon
summary: Probably in lower Salisbury Canyon. T 9 N, R 26 W.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 2)

name: Camp — Lower Santa Barbara Canyon
summary: In lower Santa Barbara Canyon. T 9 N, R 25 W.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note (cont.))

name: Sgene — Lower Cuyama Valley
summary: Party bore more to the north and found Sgene. T 10 N, R 25 W.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 3)

name: Malapoa — Bitterwater / Santiago Creek
summary: Located by Gifford & Schenck on Bitterwater or Santiago Cr. Identified as Tlamini (Kroeber).
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 4)

name: Sispetis — SE Buena Vista Lake
summary: Considered to be Pohatan Tinliu, at the SE corner of Buena Vista Lake.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 4: Kroeber (1925, pl. 47))

name: Buenasvista — 8 Leagues North
summary: Eight leagues north of Bitterwater/Santiago Cr.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 5)

name: Tejon / El Paso Creek — Foothills
summary: Six leagues from Sispetis to the mouth of Tejon or El Paso Cr. at the edge of the foothills. July 26 reconnaissance explored El Paso, Tejon, and Pastoria creeks.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 6)

name: Tupal — Tejon Ranch on El Paso Creek
summary: Placed doubtfully by Gifford & Schenck at Tejon Ranch on El Paso Cr.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 6)

name: Tacui — Tecuya Creek
summary: Party doubled back west past Grapevine Cr. to Tacui, undoubtedly on Tecuya Cr.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 7)

name: Yagueleme — Kern River / Woilo
summary: Nine leagues north of Tecuya, on the Kern R. Gifford & Schenck identify as Woilo, very close to 9 leagues from Tecuya.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 8)

name: Eastern End of Kern Lake
summary: The eastern end of Kern Lake.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 9)

name: Tashlpj — Pastoria / Grapevine Creek
summary: Cook thinks more likely Pastoria on Grapevine Cr. rather than Tejon Cr., since the party arrived at Castaic at the head of Grapevine Cr. on August 7.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 10)

name: Castaic — Head of Grapevine Creek
summary: Party arrived August 7. Beginning the crossing south.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions

name: Antelope Valley
summary: Crossed into Antelope Valley.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 11)

name: San Gabriel Mountains Crossing
summary: Party crossed the mountains and went southwest. Several villages of Serrano Indians seen.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 12)

name: Mission San Gabriel
summary: Arrival at San Gabriel Mission.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 12)

name: Mission San Juan Bautista
summary: Departure, late September 1806. Fr. Pedro Muñoz accompanying as chaplain.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions

name: San Benito River Camp
summary: 1½ leagues from the mission, on the San Benito R.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 13: 'not on Pacheco Cr., as stated by Cutter')

name: San Luis Ranch — Pacheco Pass
summary: Camp approximately at San Luis Ranch, where Highway 152 crosses San Luis Cr.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 14)

name: Santa Rita Camp
summary: 15 to 20 mi east of San Luis Gonzaga. Near Salt Slough or Paso Slough, 5–6 mi west of the main San Joaquin.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 15: 'The sloughs and channels of the San Joaquin are difficult to locate with precision on a modern map')

name: Camp on the San Joaquin
summary: On the San Joaquin R. T 8 S, R 11 E.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 16: villages effaced so thoroughly that modern informants gave no indication they ever existed)

name: Bear Creek
summary: Rather than Mariposa Cr. Approximately 3 leagues south of the Merced R.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 18: 'Cutter thinks this was Bear Cr... I see no reason to disagree')

name: Merced River — West of Livingston
summary: Party which went north reached the Merced R. somewhere west of Livingston, probably at T 6 S, R 11 E.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 19)

name: Dry Creek
summary: Undoubtedly Dry Cr. 'The description is valid even today.'
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 21)

name: Tuolumne River Area
summary: Villages seen on the Tuolumne but were deserted. Oak forest environment.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook notes 20, 24: Taulames village between the Tuolumne and Stanislaus)

name: Stanislaus River — Near Modesto
summary: Remnants of the oak forest can still be seen, extending from the junction with the San Joaquin eastward. Both sides of the river.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 22: 'if it continued in a northwesterly direction from near Modesto')

name: Upper Stanislaus — Knights Ferry Area
summary: Six leagues upstream. Vicinity of Ripon, Riverbank, or Knights Ferry. Limestone bluffs, precipitous. The best guess is the vicinity of Knights Ferry.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 23)

name: Calaveras River
summary: First stream (Rio de ... Franciscos) identified as the Calaveras.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 25: 'There is little reason to disagree')

name: Cosumnes River — Northernmost Point
summary: Second stream identified as the Cosumnes. Linguistic border between Miwok and Maidu. The diarist fails to mention the Mokelumne.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 25: northernmost extent of the expedition)

name: Cosumnes River — Turn South
summary: Party turns south, staying close to the eastern edge of the valley along the Sierra foothills.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions

name: Stanislaus / Tuolumne Area (return)
summary: Passing back through the Stanislaus and Tuolumne country.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions

name: Chowchilla / Mariposa Creek
summary: Santo Domingo identified as Mariposa Cr.; Tecolote as the Chowchilla (Cutter).
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 26)

name: Fresno River (Santa Ana)
summary: The Santa Ana was the Fresno R.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 27)

name: San Joaquin at Pizcache
summary: Pizcache on the San Joaquin, listed in the appendix. Moraga probably halted approximately north of Fresno, below Le Grand. Distinct absence of permanent Indian settlements on seasonal streams.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 27)

name: Pitkachi Village Area
summary: Pitkachi 'held the south side of the San Joaquin, living at Kohou, near Herndon or Sycamore; at Weshiu, on a slough; and at Gewachu, still farther downstream.' Village was in the mountains or at least the foothills.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 28: Gayton / Kroeber disagree on exact location)

name: Kings River — Near Sanger
summary: Kings R. was reached near Sanger or Centerville.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 31)

name: Kaweah Delta — Visalia / Telame
summary: 28th day: scouts reached the great oak forest along the Kaweah delta at or above Visalia. 29th day (Oct. 19): main party arrived. The large village of 600 souls — Telame (the Telamni). 22 persons baptized.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 33: 'The water evidently was very low — somewhat unusual for this region, even in October')

name: Tule River — Near Porterville
summary: After a winding course for two days, the party camped on the Tule R. near the foothills, probably not far from Porterville.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 36)

name: Deer Creek
summary: Probably Deer Cr.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 37)

name: White River
summary: Probably White R.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 38)

name: Poso Creek
summary: No distances given, but from the description the most likely stream is Poso Cr.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 39)

name: Kern River — Above Bakersfield
summary: The Kern R., probably some miles above Bakersfield. A 'long trip' downstream brought them into the slough country south of Bakersfield.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 40)

name: Kern River — Downstream / Slough Country
summary: South of Bakersfield. Moraga did not get within sight of Buena Vista Lake, to the west.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook notes 40–41)

name: Grapevine Canyon
summary: Zalvidea had left an account of the villages in this area from his Aug. 1806 expedition.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 41)

name: Tejon Pass
summary: On the last three days the party left the valley by way of Grapevine Canyon, over Tejon Pass.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 42: 'Cutter says the Santa Susanna Range')

name: Mission San Fernando
summary: Arrival at San Fernando Mission. End of the 1806 expedition.
sources: Muñoz/Zalvidea diaries via Cook, Colonial Expeditions (Cook note 42)
